data.title: Pandora to replace silver jewellery with platinum as prices go haywire
data.description:
Pandora plans to sell less silver jewellery and shift to platinum instead to shield itself from volatile prices.

The world’s largest jewellery maker, known for its charm bracelets, said it wanted to move away from silver following weeks of volatility in precious metal prices.

Berta de ‍Pablos-Barbier, Pandora’s new chief executive, said around 60pc of the company’s products were currently made of silver but it wanted to reduce that to “maybe 20pc in the end”.

Silver prices have swung wildly in recent weeks after Donald Trump’s unpredictable foreign policy prompted investors to flee into precious metals.

It was worth about £26 an ounce at the start of January before rocketing to a high of £88 an ounce by Jan 29, only to plummet to £58 an ounce in recent days.

This has proved a headache for Pandora because silver is used in the bulk of its products, although the company hedges about 70pc of its exposure to the precious metal.

Platinum has also seen prices rise and fall violently but the company hopes making more jewellery using the metal will help it diversify away from its reliance on silver.

Ms de ‍Pablos-Barbier said Pandora was “a jewellery brand, not a silver trader”.

Pandora on Wednesday warned it could face its first sales drop since the pandemic with the company predicting a small fall in organic sales at worst or just 2pc growth at best.

The company said its platinum jewellery was developed “to outperform silver in everyday wear” and had tested well with consumers.

Switching to platinum is likely to increase Pandora’s price tags significantly, with products sold by rival jewellers suggesting platinum jewellery can be twice as expensive as silver.

On Tiffany & Co, a narrow sterling silver ring is listed for £335 compared to £765 for a simple platinum wedding band.

Tarvier sells sterling silver rings for up to £74 while an equivalent platinum ring can cost up to £1,694.

In commodity markets, platinum trades for about £1,700 an ounce against around £58 an ounce for silver.

Pandora had already put prices up by 5pc in October, which it cited as the main driver behind an increased profitability gain in the last three months of 2025.

But the company warned that volatile gold and silver prices represented a “material headwind” to profit forecasts.

Standard Chartered analysts said precious metal volatility would likely continue “until there is greater certainty on the monetary policy outlook”.

data.title: UK gets another streaming service as HBO bets on Harry Potter series to win subscribers
data.description:
Britons who want to watch the upcoming TV adaptation of Harry Potter and rewatch The Sopranos will be forced to fork out for yet another streaming service.

The Harry Potter series – which will premiere next year and run for a decade – will only be available on HBO Max, a new streaming service slated to launch in the UK and Ireland next month.

HBO confirmed Max will be available for a starting price of £4.99 per month for a basic package with adverts, rising to £14.99 for the premium tier.

Subscribers who also want to watch TNT Sports, which has the rights to some Premier League and FA Cup football matches, will have to pay £30.99 per month, either as a standalone plan or in addition to entertainment tiers.

TNT Sports subscribers who currently access the service through Discovery+ will automatically be transferred to HBO Max.

The new streaming service, which will launch on March 26, will offer subscribers access to hit HBO series ranging from Succession and The White Lotus to The Sopranos and Game of Thrones. The platform will also carry Friends, a property owned by HBO’s parent company, Warner Bros.

Film fans will also be able to watch Warner Bros blockbusters, including recent Oscar nominees One Battle After Another and Sinners, as well as classics such as the Harry Potter and Lord of the Rings franchises.

JB Perrette, head of global streaming and games at Warner Bros Discovery, described the upcoming Harry Potter show as the “streaming event of the decade” and said HBO Max was designed to be a premium service focused on quality over quantity.

He said: “What you hear more and more from consumers is the majority of people saying: ‘I’ve just spent 30 minutes looking for something and there’s nothing good’.

“Our antidote to that is HBO Max, which is we don’t promise to say we’re going to give something for everyone and we don’t promise to say we’re going to serve every targeted niche. But what you will find on the service is everything that is done at a very high quality that is category-defining.

“That differentiation of quality and having something great at all times, as opposed to just a lot, is what makes us distinct.”

Warner Bros takeover

The launch comes despite deep uncertainty over the future of HBO’s parent company Warner Bros, which is currently in the throes of a blockbuster Hollywood takeover battle.

Warner Bros has agreed an $83bn (£60bn) deal with Netflix but is fighting off a rival $108bn bid from Paramount, the media group controlled by the billionaire Ellison family.

The planned merger with Netflix has sparked competition concerns and uncertainty over the future of HBO Max.

However, Mr Perrette insisted that Netflix had an “enormous amount of appreciation, respect and value the HBO brand”.

The launch of HBO Max in the UK is a setback to Sky, which will lose exclusive access to hit shows.

The basic ad-supported version of HBO Max will be bundled into Sky subscriptions, meaning viewers will still be able to watch new releases. However, Sky will no longer carry back catalogues of older series such as The Sopranos on its own channels and on-demand service.

Sky previously sued Warner Bros for hundreds of millions of dollars in damages, alleging the US company cut it out of the new Harry Potter series. The lawsuit was voluntarily dismissed in late 2024.

HBO Max first launched in the US in 2020 and is now available in 110 countries. Overall, Warner Bros Discovery has around 128 million streaming subscribers worldwide.

data.title: Ford falls behind BYD as China upends car industry
data.description:
Ford has been overtaken in sales for the first time by Chinese carmaker BYD in a sign of the changes upending the automotive industry.

The US motor giant’s sales fell by 2pc to just under 4.4 million last year. That compares to 4.6 million sold over the same period by BYD, which climbed to sixth in the rankings of global car manufacturers.

Ford’s US sales rose but the carmaker has been losing ground in both Europe and China.

The company is best-known for top-selling cars such as the Ford Fiesta and the Ford Focus, which remain among the most-driven vehicles on Britain’s roads.

BYD overtaking Ford is a landmark moment due to the US giant’s historic role in shaping the industry, with founder Henry Ford’s Model T ushering in the modern era of car ownership in the early 20th century.

American car giants and other Western carmakers face growing competition from the likes of BYD and other Chinese brands that are low-priced and increasingly high-tech.

BYD’s most popular cars include its SEAL U DM-i and the Dolphin Surf electric city car, which sells for less than £19,000.

Ford phased out the Fiesta during the pandemic and has since gravitated towards more expensive crossovers and SUVs, with its cheapest car, the Puma, starting at just over £26,000.

However, the US company remains Britain’s third-biggest car brand with more than double the UK sales of BYD last year.

It sold about 119,000 thousands cars in 2025, representing a market share of 5.9pc, according to the Society for Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT).

That was an increase of 8pc on the previous year.

By comparison, BYD sold about 51,400 cars and took a market share of about 2.5pc – but its sales increased nearly sixfold.

Difficult EV shift

Ford and its Western rivals have struggled to adapt to the electric vehicle (EV) transition.

The company announced a $19.5bn (£14bn) hit to its balance sheet in December to scale back EV production, blaming lacklustre consumer demand.

Felipe Munoz, an independent automotive analyst, contrasted Ford’s difficulties and slow sales growth in regions such as Europe with BYD’s rapid expansion worldwide, which has seen the Chinese company increasingly focus on exports to regions such as Europe and South America.

This is partly because of intense competition and price wars between Chinese brands at home, although the country’s government has sought to wind back state support and discourage cut-throat tactics.

Mr Munoz said: “BYD is still in expansion mode and, even if their sales in China are not performing as well as in the past, they’re now relying on exports to grow.

“Ford has not really expanded, it’s still in the same markets and 50pc of the sales are happening in the US, a market where growth isn’t the best.

“Then you have Europe that doesn’t grow either, and only a minor presence in China. So basically, this is something we expected, and is something that should continue to be the case this year, because BYD is growing thanks to the export markets.”

He added that Ford’s troubles with electrification were magnified because of its dependence on North America, where government see-sawing on support and poor consumer demand had taken a toll.

“That doesn’t help, because the North Americans are not enthusiastic about electric cars,” Mr Munoz said.

“And Ford cannot make use of the strong demand for electric cars in China, either.”

In China, Ford has launched an all-electric version of its Bronco SUV in a joint venture with Jiangling Motors – but its market share in the country has collapsed from nearly 5pc a decade ago to less than 2pc today.

Mr Munoz said: “Let’s see how the new Ford Bronco Electric performs in China. But so far, nothing has happened, not even in Europe, with these electric cars.”

Japan’s Toyota remained number one for the sixth year in a row, with global sales of 11.3 million.

Ford was approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Hinkley Point C’s ‘fish disco’ actually works, researchers find
data.description:
An underwater “fish disco” at Britain’s newest nuclear power station has been mocked as an example of the huge cost and bureaucracy involved with building things in the UK.

But at least the new system, designed to preserve fish species, actually works.

EDF, the developer behind Hinkley Point C plant in Somerset, on Tuesday said trials of the acoustic fish deterrent – underwater speakers that blast out sound to keep fish away, dubbed the “fish disco” – found it was “highly effective”.

Tests at sea and in laboratory tanks by scientists at Swansea University showed that only a single tagged twait shad came within 98 feet of the device when it was switched on, compared to 14 when it was off.

This suggested “an effectiveness of more than 90pc”, EDF said, although further trials will be carried out later this year.

The fish disco forms part of £700m of fish protection measures planned for the nuclear power plant, which EDF said gave the site more marine conservation capability than any comparable site in the world.

The findings by Swansea University were welcomed by activists, who said the results proved that ministers should not water down environmental standards for nuclear power.

This week, a cross-party group of some 60 backbench MPs, including more than 30 from the Labour benches, joined nature charities to warn that any relaxation of standards would trigger “unnecessary and hugely damaging environmental regression”.

However, they are being opposed by pro-growth campaigners and industry experts who have pointed to a government review that found nuclear developers faced a “fragmented” regulatory system that often imposed “disproportionate” demands with little regard to costs or trade-offs.

Hinkley’s cooling pipes will suck in around 132,000 litres of water per second from the Severn Estuary to cool the plant’s two nuclear reactors once they are operational.

According to the Environment Agency, this risks putting more than four million fish at risk of being killed. These include Atlantic salmon, herring, European sea bass and European eels, which are critically endangered.

To mitigate the plant’s impact, EDF said it had spent £700m on fish protection measures, including a special intake pipe mouth costing £500m, a fish recovery and return system for £150m and an “acoustic fish deterrent” costing another £50m.

The French state-owned energy giant said the effectiveness of these measures meant it should not have to also create a 900-acre salt-marsh nearby.

Critics of the fish protection measures at Hinkley have branded them “regulatory madness”, arguing they have added huge costs and delays to the project for disproportionately small benefits.

A review by the economist John Fingleton found the fish protection measures would save “0.083 salmon per year” along with “0.028 sea trout, six river lamprey, 18 Allis shad, and 528 twaite shad”.

Sam Richards, of pro-growth campaign group Britain Remade, said: “Blowing £700m on fish protection for Hinkley Point C, including the now-legendary ‘fish disco’, is indefensible in the extreme.

“This is just the latest piece of research that highlights the huge costs and delays Britain’s planning system and habit regulations foist on major infrastructure projects, for little gain.”

Mr Richards also pointed to findings by Swansea University that salmon identified as at risk in fact avoid the Hinkley site.

Salmon migrating to the Atlantic generally used the main channel “well away from Hinkley Point C”, researchers said. In two years, only two tagged salmon were detected within a kilometre of the intake pipes.

Mr Richards said: “After years of delay and hundreds of millions spent, we’re told fish barely go near the intakes at Hinkley anyway. This isn’t environmentalism, it’s regulatory madness.”

However, Matt Browne, of the Wildlife Trusts, defended the measures and accused opponents of misrepresenting the number of fish saved.

Mr Browne said: “We are happy with the acoustic fish deterrent, combined with the other two measures.

“What we would like to see is for EDF to stop belly-aching. This is a hugely important stretch of water for fish, including protected species, and yet there have been persistent suggestions that somehow this is all unnecessary.”

Some 50,000 endangered eels alone were at risk of being killed each year without the measures, Mr Browne said.

He added: “We say this is good news – it is effective mitigation and an example of the rules working as they should do.”

Ministers are expected to set out plans for simplifying nuclear regulations in the coming weeks.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ocado prepares to scrap 1,000 jobs in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Ocado is preparing to cut as many as 1,000 jobs as part of a scramble to drive down costs.

The struggling company, which employs around 20,000 staff, is preparing to reduce headcount to save hundreds of millions of pounds.

The proposed redundancies could be approved later this month, with those in its head office most likely to be affected. However, a final decision has yet to be made.

The job cuts are being made by Ocado Group, the London-listed company that builds robot warehouses and owns 50pc of online grocery business Ocado Retail.

It comes after a turbulent year for the business, which has seen its share price fall more than 40pc in the last six months alone.

This has been blamed on the loss of crucial warehouse contracts in North America.

A source close to the business claimed the proposed job losses form part of a strategy to improve cash flow over the next two years.

The company made a loss of £375m in 2024 but swung to a profit in the first half of last year on the revaluation of a stake in Ocado Retail, the groceries business it runs jointly with M&S.

Although best known for its grocery deliveries to British households, the company also pioneered the extensive use of robots in its warehouses, a technology it offers to businesses worldwide.

However, Ocado’s shares were rocked last year after US retailer Kroger confirmed plans to close three automated warehouses that used the company’s technology.

Kroger, which has built eight sites with Ocado since 2018, said it was moving to fulfil some of its online orders from existing stores instead of using the warehouses.

Another blow came last month when Sobeys, a supermarket in Canada, announced it would shut another site using the British company’s kit.

An Ocado spokesman said: “We regularly review our operations to ensure we’re set up for long-term success. If and when decisions are made that affect our people, we are committed to communicating with them directly and ensuring they are supported throughout.”

Ocado’s potential job losses, first reported by The Times, come at a time of darkening fortunes for those seeking work in the UK, fuelled by falling vacancies and rising redundancies, which have seen unemployment rise to a four-year high of 5.1pc.

Demand for staff fell in January for the 27th consecutive month, recruiters warned, with particularly large falls in vacancies for medical, secretarial, hospitality and retail workers.

The figures from the Recruitment and Employment Confederation and KPMG raise fears that unemployment will continue to rise, despite Government pledges to boost employment and wages.

“A growing, inclusive economy requires high levels of employment – a focus on encouraging firms to create jobs rather than discouraging that investment is more important than ever,” said Neil Carberry, chief executive of the REC.

“So far, the Government has struggled to convince businesses that it wants them to hire. That has to change in the decisions that are made this year if we are to avoid a continued rise in unemployment.”

A Government spokesman said: “This Government is creating the conditions businesses need to recruit and grow by investing £1.5bn in work, training and apprenticeships while restoring economic stability and bearing down on inflation, helping interest rates to fall six times, giving firms the confidence to hire.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Shell chief exec to become one of FTSE’s best paid bosses
data.description:
Shell’s chief executive is on course to become one of the best-paid executives on the London Stock Exchange as his company’s move away from green energy pays off.

Wael Sawan could see his total pay packet jump by £4.5m to a maximum £19m a year under proposals drawn up by the FTSE 100 company. That would make him one of the country’s highest paid blue chip executives.

Other high-earners include AstraZeneca’s Pascal Soriot, who was paid £15m in 2024, and Rolls-Royce’s Tufan Erginbilgic, whose pay is reportedly being increased to a maximum of £18m. Simon Peckham, Melrose’s chief executive, took home £58m upon retiring last year.

Mr Sawan’s base salary is just over £1.5m and the changes would mean he could earn nine times that amount per year in long-term performance pay, up from a maximum of six times currently.

That would make him eligible to receive stock awards worth £13.8m, up from £9m previously. On top of that, he can receive a maximum annual bonus of £3.8m – taking his total potential package to more than £19m.

Under Sawan, Shell has cut its exposure to green energy and focused its attention on more profitable oil and gas operations.

In November, Shell confirmed it was ditching its only two UK wind farm projects – MarramWind farm and CampionWind off the east coast of Scotland – after carrying out a business review.

Earlier in 2025, the company also revealed plans to ramp up gas sales while reducing the share of wind and solar in its power generation portfolio from 50pc to 20pc by 2030.

Shell is focusing investment in gas-fired power plants and grid-scale batteries instead, with bosses also planning to maintain oil and gas output at current levels through the end of the decade. Investors have cheered the focus on fossil fuels.

The company is already the world’s largest producer of liquefied natural gas (LNG).

Shares in the company have jumped 22pc since Mr Sawan took over in January 2023. That compares to a 0.1pc increase at BP, a 33pc increase at ExxonMobil and a 1.2pc increase at Chevron over the same period.

Mr Sawan’s proposed pay deal would still be far smaller than those of his US rivals. Darren Woods, the boss of Exxon, received $44.1m (£32.2m) last year, while Chevron boss Mike Wirth made $32.7m.

On Wednesday, a Shell spokesman said: “Every three years, Shell seeks shareholder approval for a new executive director remuneration policy as a standard part of regulations for UK-listed companies. 

“The last vote was in 2023, so this is part of the usual cycle. Final proposals will be published in the 2025 annual report on March 12 and voted on at the annual general meeting.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BT slows customer exodus as ‘alt-net’ rivals run into trouble
data.description:
BT has managed to slow an exodus of broadband customers as struggling “alt-net” rivals begin to run into trouble.

The former monopoly shed 210,000 accounts in the last three months of 2025, a slight improvement on the 240,000 lost in the previous three months.

Overall, BT said it expected its broadband customer numbers to fall by 850,000 for the full year, rather than the 900,000 it had previously forecast.

The telecoms giant has been struggling to keep hold of broadband customers as it faces fierce competition from so-called “alt-net” rivals offering cut-price deals.

Scores of these alt-nets have sprung up to challenge BT’s dominance by borrowing heavily to build their own full-fibre networks.

The issue is understood to be one of the key areas of concern for Sunil Bharti Mittal, the Indian billionaire who is now BT’s largest shareholder and has taken a seat on the board.

The latest figures suggest Ms Kirkby, who is entering her third year at the helm of BT, may be making progress on the issue, which would help to ease pressure on her.

The drop in broadband losses comes as a sharp increase in interest costs and lukewarm demand pushes many “alt-nets” into crisis.

London-focused G Network was plunged into administration last month after being snapped up by the private equity firm FitzWalter Capital. A wave of consolidation is also expected, with Virgin Media O2 finalising a £2bn takeover deal for Netomnia that will add three million homes to its footprint.

Under Ms Kirkby, BT has pledged to cut heavy investment in its full-fibre broadband network, strip out £3bn of costs and increase returns to shareholders.

While investors initially welcomed Ms Kirkby’s strategy, she is now facing calls to demonstrate growth and define the behemoth’s place in an increasingly competitive market.

BT, which is celebrating its 180th anniversary this year and also owns the EE mobile network, is expected to launch a major brand refresh in the coming months that will emphasise the group’s heritage.

The company said on Thursday that it connected a further one million homes to its full-fibre broadband network last quarter – putting it on track to meet its target of 25 million by the end of this year.

However, revenue fell 4pc to just under £5bn as a result of lower income from customers and weak demand for mobile handsets. Pre-tax profit slumped by £244m to £183m on the back of losses for TNT Sports, BT’s joint venture with Warner Bros Discovery.

Ms Kirkby said: “BT continues to deliver on its strategy – building and connecting the UK to the best next-generation networks at record pace, while accelerating our transformation.”

Vodafone shares slump

Separately, Vodafone reported a 7.3pc rise in group service revenue to €8.5bn (£7.3bn) in the third quarter as it cashed in on higher revenue from its £15bn UK merger with Three.

However, growth remained sluggish in the company’s key market of Germany at just 0.7pc amid a difficult turnaround. Shares in Vodafone fell 6pc.

Margherita Della Valle, chief executive of Vodafone, said: “After a fast start, we are making very good progress with the integration of our UK business. Looking ahead, we are on track to deliver at the upper end of our guidance range for both profit and cash flow.”

data.title: Barclays cuts jobs in AI and offshoring drive
data.description:
Barclays has put dozens of London jobs at risk with plans to move roles to India and use artificial intelligence (AI).

The British bank is planning to cut up to 50 staff from its internal advertising unit, moving the jobs offshore by the end of August.

Barclays will cut costs by slashing the jobs in London and rehiring a new team in India, where they will be supported by AI tools.

The cuts mark the latest effort by CS Venkatakrishnan, the chief executive of Barclays, to reduce spending as part of plans to cut the bank’s total costs by £2bn.

As part of the shake-up, Barclays plans to cut the entirety of its almost 20-strong copywriting team, which works out of the bank’s headquarters in Canary Wharf.

Marketing workers were first informed of the overhaul at a meeting last July, attended by around 50 employees across the division whose jobs were put at risk by the initiative.

Barclays’ new copywriting team in India will be larger than its current one in London. However, the move is expected to result in reduced staffing costs overall due to lower wages. 

Barclays currently has over 30,000 employees in India, with offices in cities including Mumbai and New Delhi.

Staff working from India will be expected to use AI to help them in their jobs, using the technology to write public-facing material for Barclays including advertising copy for the bank’s website.

Some staff inside the bank’s London-based marketing division have already been made redundant and left last year. Others will be offered jobs elsewhere in the bank, while some expect to have their contracts ended over the coming months.

The emergence of AI technologies has led to wider concerns about jobs, with the launch of a new legal chatbot by Anthropic last week causing a major downturn in stocks worldwide because of fears that major companies could be undercut by AI-powered rivals.

Software developers experienced sharp drops in their share prices amid concerns that demand for their products could fall if their customers start using cheaper AI tools instead. Advertising companies were also hit on fears much of their work could be automated.

Britain is currently suffering the worst impacts of the AI-driven disruption, with a study from Morgan Stanley in January showing the technology has led to the loss of more jobs than it has created in the UK.

Barclays’ wider cuts, announced in 2024, saw the bank vow to return £10bn to its shareholders via a mixture of dividends and share buyback initiatives.

Barclays’ share price has since increased by more than 180pc. The stock was lifted by strong results after it successfully capitalised on a combination of higher interest rates and global market turmoil caused by Donald Trump’s policies.

Barclays cut 200 of its investment bankers across the business last June as part of the £2bn cost-cutting plans.

Its profits were also boosted by its £600m takeover of Tesco Bank in November 2024. The deal meant Barclays added 3.8 million of the UK lender’s retail customers.

Mr Venkatakrishnan, who is known as Venkat inside the bank, later saw his pay packet more than double in February 2025, from £4.6m in 2024 to £10.5m.

Barclays is one of the UK’s “big four” banks alongside HSBC, Lloyds Bank and NatWest. It is one of Britain’s oldest banks and traces its origins back to the founding of a small firm established by two Quaker financiers in 1690.

A Barclays spokesman said: “It is incorrect to report the copywriting roles in one of our marketing teams are being replaced by AI.

“These specific roles are being relocated to India. At Barclays, we are using AI as a tool to enhance human capability and people continue to play a crucial role in the development and delivery of our marketing strategy.”

data.title: Britain’s energy suppliers are still in a worryingly shaky state
data.description:
Four years on from the energy crisis that triggered the collapse of around half of UK suppliers, is our power system more secure?

You might think the answer is yes, after Ofgem introduced new rules intended to make sure energy companies are more financially resilient should future shocks occur.

But the energy regulator has confirmed that five suppliers are failing to meet its new capital requirements, up from three last year.

It has not named the suppliers. However, both Octopus Energy and Ovo Energy have both publicly confirmed they are not meeting the requirements. Both companies have recently agreed to capitalisation plans to help them meet the target.

Much smaller supplier Utilita also reported difficulties meeting the target due to seasonal demand fluctuations, but said it was short-lived and they soon went back above the target.

Under rules introduced in March 2025, suppliers are now expected to hold net assets, excluding intangibles such as goodwill, of £115 for every dual fuel customer they have, and half that sum for every customer on an individual fuel contract.

The logic of setting this spare cash aside is simple: suppliers should have enough balance sheet resilience to survive shocks in wholesale gas markets without failing and leaving consumers to pick up the tab.

Significant costs are incurred when a supplier fails, many of which are recovered through a levy on all energy bills.

In 2021-22, when half the suppliers failed, these costs ran into the billions and were loaded onto bills at a time when households were already struggling with high energy prices.

The biggest cost arises from credit balances: if a customer is in credit with a supplier that fails, the new energy company that takes on that customer must honour that balance. The “lifeboat” supplier is reimbursed for that cost through a levy on all our bills.

Another major cost is hedging – any gas or electricity purchased in advance by the failed supplier is treated as an asset in insolvency and used to repay creditors.

It cannot legally be transferred to the supplier that takes on the customers, meaning they have to go and buy contracts to cover themselves. That is another expense added to the levy.

The regime has not been without controversy. Centrica, owner of British Gas, has argued that suppliers should ring-fence customer credit balances so they are not mixed with the supplier’s own money and used to fund day-to-day operations.

Most other suppliers lobbied hard against it, and Ofgem ultimately declined to mandate ring-fencing, discouraging the use of credit balances as working capital without requiring a hard separation.

Ofgem sets out a range of restrictions it can impose on suppliers that are not on track to meet the requirement. In serious cases, it can require full ring-fencing of customer balances and restrict new customer acquisition.

The regulator does not name the suppliers with capitalisation plans nor detail anything about the plans themselves and progress towards meeting them.

The logic is that publicly naming them could create a “run on the bank” situation, where customers quit for fear of losing their power supply, (even though the regulatory regime ensures this would not happen even in the event of a failure).

This lack of transparency is increasingly hard to defend given the size of the two suppliers we know to be on the list. Octopus is the UK’s biggest supplier, with more than seven million customers, and Ovo has around four million customers.

There is no suggestion that either business is in financial trouble or at risk of going bust, but the scale of these businesses means serious thought must be given to the possibility.

Both are far bigger than Bulb, which had 1.7 million customers when it collapsed in November 2021. Bulb was considered too large for a conventional supplier of last resort process and required an expensive special administration regime to ensure customers were not left without power.

In banking, capital ratios and risk-weighted assets of major firms are published every quarter by the Bank of England to maintain confidence. Why not in the energy sector too?

Energy consumers are exposed in a similar way: their credit balances are effectively guaranteed, just as deposits are protected under the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. 

If we accept that supplier failures impose system-wide costs, then transparency should be a core part of the prudential regime, not an optional extra.

The primary role of suppliers is to handle customer money and do some accounting, which is essentially what retail banks do. 

If Ofgem’s experiment in regulation fails, it will be hard to defend its continued responsibility for the retail supply market.

data.title: NatWest chief calls for stability amid Starmer leadership crisis
data.description:
Natwest’s chief executive has called for stability from the Government as Sir Keir Starmer’s premiership is left hanging in the balance.

On Monday, Paul Thwaite said the bank was seeking “certainty and growth” from Labour as the Prime Minister fights for his political survival. The resignation of Morgan McSweeney, a key ally of the PM, has raised fears about a leadership challenge.

UK borrowing costs rose and the pound fell on Monday amid the turmoil.

Mr McSweeney resigned on Sunday after taking “full responsibility” for urging Sir Keir to appoint Lord Mandelson as US ambassador despite fears over the peer’s links to Jeffrey Epstein.

Asked about the turmoil gripping the Government, Mr Thwaite said British business wanted to see a period of stability from Westminster.

“What all businesses need is stability, certainty and growth,” he said. “They’re the conditions that create the right environment, so that’s what I would be looking for, that’s what any business leader would be looking for.”

NatWest swoops for City firm

The comments came after NatWest sealed its largest acquisition in nearly 20 years, buying leading British wealth manager Evelyn Partners in a major bet on the UK economy.

Mr Thwaite said the takeover still “made sense” irrespective of the political or economic environment.

“My perspective is this is a long-term strategic move and what I want, irrespective of government, is stability, certainty and growth,” he said.

The bank, which was owned by taxpayers until last year, acquired Evelyn as it seeks to boost money management services for its 20 million customers.

The FTSE 100 lender will pay £2.7bn for Evelyn, making it the largest deal since before the height of the financial crisis in 2008.

Paul Thwaite, the bank’s chief executive, said: “Bringing together these two leading businesses creates a unique opportunity to provide financial planning, savings and investment services to more families and people across the UK.

“At a time when the benefits of saving and investing are increasingly part of the national conversation, we can help customers to make more of their money through a broader range of services, as well as helping to drive growth and investment across the economy.”

The takeover is the largest since NatWest, then known as Royal Bank of Scotland (RBS), paid €27bn (£23.5bn) to acquire Dutch lender ABN Amro at the height of the financial crisis.

Shareholders reacted badly to the deal, sending Natwest shares down more than 7pc to 612p. 

Banks have increasingly sought to move into the financial planning industry, hoping to tap the large amount of savings from customers to earn more from fees.

Evelyn was created in 2020 from a merger of Tilney and Smith and Williamson, which dated back to the 19th century, by owners Permira and Warburg Pincus. It has £69bn of assets under management and nearly 156,000 customers.

Competing with Lloyds

Analysts from Citi said the deal would help NatWest compete more aggressively with Lloyds.

“When comparing NatWest to Lloyds, one of the criticisms we receive of NatWest is that it lacks a non-net interest income growth story and this acquisition would go some way to resolving this,” they said.

NatWest already has a significant presence in wealth management through its ownership of upmarket private bank Coutts.

The takeover is expected to boost NatWest’s fee income by around 20pc.

The acquisition will also more than double the value of NatWest’s £59bn in assets under management and administration, lifting the total to £127bn.

NatWest said it expected the deal will lead to £100m worth of cost savings in the immediate term and said it aimed to achieve £150m of cost savings in the future.

NatWest was taken under state control in the wake of the 2008 financial crash but was finally returned to private ownership when the Government sold its last shares in May last year.

The bank’s share price has risen sharply over the past two years as it has profited from higher interest rates.

NatWest is one of Britain’s “big four” banks alongside HSBC, Lloyds and Barclays. It traces its origins back to 1658 with the founding of Smith’s Bank in Nottingham. The bank also announced a £750m share buyback in addition to the takeover.

data.title: Driverless cars take black cabs’ charging bays
data.description:
London’s taxi drivers have clashed with driverless car company Waymo after its vehicles were found taking up electric charging bays reserved for black cabs.

Taxi groups said there had been multiple cases of Waymo’s electric vehicles using dedicated e-taxi points, leading to intervention by Transport for London (TfL).

Industry representatives said that taxi drivers had resorted to cancelling the self-driving cars’ charging sessions.

Steve McNamara, of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association, said he was aware of a handful of incidents and claimed that in at least one case a taxi driver had pressed a cancel button on the charge point.

“I share the frustration. It’s the equivalent of me parking cabs on petrol pumps and saying you can’t go to work,” he said.

Waymo, a spin-off from Google recently valued at $126bn (£92bn), is testing its driverless cars in London before launching services to the public later in the year.

The plans are seen as the latest challenge to London’s highly regulated black cab industry, which has strict barriers to entry such as requiring drivers to memorise the capital’s streets.

The United Cabbies Group said it had reported Waymo to TfL twice in the last two weeks, in response to a driver who shared a photo of a car charging in Paddington, west London.

TfL told the group: “The company has been very apologetic and has taken some robust action. It has also put in place some further, practical measures to prevent these vehicles using taxi-dedicated points.”

Waymo said it had raised the issue with Moove, the operations company whose workers operate its robocar fleet.

“We take our responsibility to be a good member of London’s transport network very seriously, and we apologise to the taxi drivers whose charging needs were temporarily disrupted,” the company said.

“We’re aware of two instances of this issue and have been in close communication with TfL about each. We raised this with our driving operations partner, who assured us they implemented strict policies to address this human error.”

London has around 70 rapid charging bays reserved for electric taxis, which travel up to 200 miles on a daily shift.

Penalty charge notices have previously been issued to car owners, such as minicab drivers who use dedicated taxi points.

Around two thirds of the 13,869 licensed taxis in London are zero-emission capable, according to TfL statistics.

Waymo is operating a fleet of modified electric Jaguar I-Pace vehicles in London, using human safety drivers, as it tests its technology in the capital.

The company plans to launch rides to the public as soon as September. It has said that prices will be “competitive”.

A wave of driverless car companies are expected to launch pilot programmes over the coming months, including British start-up Wayve and China’s Baidu.

TfL said it was aware of a small number of incidents and had raised them with the company.

data.title: The battle over ‘mad’ plans to redevelop Liverpool Street station
data.description:
On a cold night this week as the rain lashed down, campaigners gathered in an unassuming east London office block to thwart a £1.5bn revamp of Britain’s busiest station.

The Liverpool Street Station Campaign (Lissca) – a “supergroup” of heritage enthusiasts spearheaded by TV presenter Griff Rhys Jones – is fighting plans to demolish and rebuild key parts of the railway terminus and linked Underground station.

The group this week unveiled its alternative vision, one they claim will help to modernise Liverpool Street station without destroying its history.

The rival plan drawn up by John McAslan + Partners, the architects behind the transformation of King’s Cross station in 2012, will be presented to local officials in the coming weeks.

“If you’re coming from Italy on a train to Liverpool Street station, would you rather see a brand new gateway ... or something which says Victorian, Britain and great railway heritage?” Rhys Jones says.

“If you break it, it’s gone, you can’t get it back. It could be a much richer experience, without doing an awful lot.”

Network Rail say expansion is needed to cope with the expected increase in passenger numbers at a station already at capacity. They argue only a wholesale overhaul will do.

Campaigners, meanwhile, claim that plans to modernise the station would ruin a Victorian landmark and create years of needless disruption.

The fight over Liverpool Street station, which opened fully in 1875, is a microcosm of the tug of war between heritage and modernisation that threatens to hamper Labour’s ambitions for a construction boom.

Since taking power, Sir Keir Starmer has vowed to make it more difficult for people to oppose major building projects.

He has pledged to override the “whims of Nimbys” and back the builders rather than the “blockers”. But in cases like Liverpool Street, these statements have only galvanised the opposition.

Al Watson, head of planning at Taylor Wessing, says: “The situation is more tense now than it was four years ago because the previous government leaned more towards heritage.

“This Government, by its very nature, pre- and post-election, is pro-development. That’s the vision, that’s the instruction, and that’s why it is more tense now than it has been in a long time.”

Until now, Lissca has faced down each proposal to redevelop Liverpool Street station. The group in fact dates back to the 1970s, when British Rail’s initial bid to demolish the terminus was thwarted by Lissca, then fronted by the poet Sir John Betjeman.

More recently, plans drawn up in 2023 by architects Herzog and de Meuron were shot down after the group helped mobilise 2,200 objections and 26,000 petition signatures.

“We’ve won battle after battle,” says Sir Simon Jenkins, founder of the Railway Heritage Trust and a supporter of the group.

The new plans, ordered by Network Rail and designed by architects Acme, will involve partially demolishing the station to expand its concourse.

A 19-storey, 97m glass office tower would be built above a neighbouring listed former hotel, while gothic yellow brick vaults would arch over the station’s entrances.

Over 150 million passengers by 2041

Network Rail argues that expansion is essential.

The opening of the Elizabeth Line, which passes through Liverpool Street, has helped the station become the UK’s busiest in each of the past three years.

There were 98 million rail entries and exits last year, and Network Rail predicts this will grow to 158 million by 2041.

Planning officials for the City of London Corporation are currently reviewing the revamp proposals, with councillors expected to vote on them on Feb 10.

The scheme has so far attracted more than 2,275 letters of objection and 1,135 in favour.

The February deadline has galvanised Lissca into action, with the group hoping their alternative plans will convince the City of London to reject Network Rail’s plans.

“What we have to do over the next 20 days … is do what we can to make sure that [the committee is] aware that the City of London deserves better than this current scheme that is before them, and they have the right to ask for it to be better,” Jones says.

‘Illogical’ plans

While Network Rail’s designs will evoke the station’s Victorian heritage, Lissca believe they can retain far more of the original building.

They estimates its plans will take half the time and cost half as much as Network Rail’s proposals, with almost no demolition required.

“The reason it’s so high is because of the void costs, created by the demolition of so much fabric,” says McAslan. “It’s so illogical, it’s mad.”

Lissca’s vision includes a green walkway inspired by the Eden Project, with Sir Tim Smit, founder of the Eden Project, advising McAslan on landscape strategy.

A vaulted office building would sit at the northern end of the station’s platforms, with nine floors of timber hanging from a lightweight steel frame to provide around 700,000 sq ft of workspace.

McAslan believes Network Rail’s plans will cause 12 years of disruption for travellers – a timeframe he calls “completely unacceptable”.

But Network Rail says this timeframe is a gross exaggeration.

Work on modernising the station itself will begin as early as 2028 and be completed in about five years, with development of offices above the passenger concourse taking three more.

While ticket barriers will be moved and shops demolished to enlarge the concourse, the impact on people using the station will be limited to the “mild inconvenience” of having to use different entrances at certain times, Network Rail says.

Officials argue the project should be no more disruptive than the modernisation of Paddington at the turn of the century and far removed from the upheaval that accompanied the upgrade of London Bridge station.

The five-year, £1bn project to transform London Bridge, the capital’s oldest rail terminus, resulted in delays and dangerous levels of overcrowding.

A Network Rail spokesman said: “It’s very much not going to take 12 years and it won’t be on the scale of London Bridge because none of the work being done is to create new tracks and platforms.”

Network Rail commissioned an independent team to review McAslan’s preliminary scheme but concluded it was undeliverable owing to complexity, funding challenges and the potential for prolonged station closures.

Its review concluded that building offices over the train shed – as proposed in the McAslan plan – would be too costly and structurally challenging because Network Rail does not own land to either side.

It would also require “significant closure” of the station to build a deck over live lines.

Back in the war room at McAslan’s Farringdon offices, campaigners are undeterred.

“There’s a critical mass of buildings which create a sense of the Victorian past in that part of the city, and it must be preserved,” says Sir Simon.

“Just remember, there’s always a choice. Nothing is inevitable. Fight on.”

data.title: Gogglebox star: BT took away my landline. My life went downhill
data.description:
A Gogglebox star has said BT made her life worse by taking away her telephone landline.

Mary Killen, an etiquette expert who found fame on Channel 4’s reality TV show, said her quality of life had “gone downhill” since BT carried out a compulsory digital upgrade on her home phone system.

The telecoms giant is one of many rolling out “Digital Voice”, a system overhauling Britain’s analogue copper cables in favour of a fully digital network reliant on broadband routers.

BT and other providers had been aiming to complete the transition to a fully digital network by 2025, but the deadline was pushed back to 2027 amid safety concerns.

The rollout was paused after some customers were unable to dial 999 during Storm Eunice in 2022 as power cuts caused internet connections to stop working and mobile batteries to go flat.

Emergency health pendants and burglar alarms linked to landline infrastructure could stop working unless users upgrade their phone systems.

Ms Killen claimed BT removed her landline service altogether “without instruction or consent”.

However, a succession of engineers had failed to resolve teething issues with her new, Wi-Fi-reliant landline cutting out.

The workers are said to have blamed the lack of connection in her bedroom on the old Wiltshire cottage’s thick walls, despite her having a clear connection in the decades before.

Writing in The Spectator, she said: “For decades, I had spent many happy hours per day lying in bed (like Mrs Stitch, my role model) organising jobs and romantic partners for friends, jigsawing together the data – unearthed by long-form chatting over the copper landline – to make the matches.”

Ms Killen said her ability to matchmake had suffered as a result of the overhaul, as well as her ability to talk things through with others over the phone.

“So, without a copper landline, my quality of life has gone downhill. Not only can I no longer matchmake but I also can’t resolve things so easily,” she said.

“I like being able to think things through by talking them through. Truths emerge from extended conversations, especially when, as in the psychiatrist’s chair, there is no eye contact.”

BT and other providers had been aiming to complete the transition to a fully digital network by 2025, but the deadline was pushed back to 2027 amid safety concerns.

“Only those with landlines had any idea of what was going on in the outside world,” Ms Killen said, referring to Storm Eunice, adding: “Now, landlines of the sort which could be relied upon during national crises of the past will cease to exist.”

BT offers free battery back-up units, hybrid phones and in-home assistance but fears remain about people in remote locations being cut off.

After halting the transition following concerns raised by The Telegraph in 2022, Marc Allera, BT’s chief executive of consumer brands, apologised and conceded the firm had “underestimated the disruptive impact this upgrade would have”.

But BT said it would nevertheless press ahead with the rollout for the network had become obsolete and unreliable.

Telecom companies have argued that fibre-optic cables allow for better-quality calls, greater energy efficiency and integration of AI to better protect customers against scammers.

But Ms Killen, who is married to Giles Wood, an artist, questioned why BT should have changed a winning formula for the worse.

“The old system wasn’t broke, so why did we have to fix it?”

Brought up in Northern Ireland, Ms Killen has written several books and pens The Spectator’s agony column but found mainstream fame on Gogglebox, where viewers watch her watching TV.

A BT spokesman said: “While we were sorry to read about Mary’s experience, we can’t comment on her case without her customer details.

“We’ve attempted to get in touch with Mary but haven’t heard back. We’d encourage her to contact us so we can understand which provider she’s with and if BT can help.”

The telecoms provider said it contacts customers at least four weeks before migrating to Digital Voice.

data.title: Hard work is finally being rewarded in Britain
data.description:
It is yet another example of corporate greed. It is fuelling inequality. And the fat cats are getting richer while the rest of the country suffers.

The attack lines from the Left on the £15m bonus that Barclays awarded to its chief executive CS Venkatakrishnan on Tuesday will write themselves.

But for once, perhaps, we should stop tearing business leaders down, and celebrate success instead. Venkat, as the Barclays boss is usually known, has shown us that hard work is finally being rewarded in Britain – and we desperately need a few more chief executives with the same kind of drive, ability and vision.

Prime minister-in-waiting Angela Rayner, along with her chancellor-in-waiting Ed Miliband, are probably preparing the announcement already. A one-off “fat cats levy” will be imposed, set at 90pc of anything paid over the minimum wage, with the money to be spent on our NHS and green energy.

Barclays has certainly made its chief executive a target with his latest pay award. He has been awarded a 29pc increase in his compensation, with his total package for the year rising from £11.6m to just over £15m. It is the largest payout Barclays has made since the £17m that was provocatively awarded to Bob Diamond, the swashbuckling American, way back in 2011. The big pay packets in the City are back.

Of course, there will be plenty of people arguing the “banksters” are back in charge and that bonuses need to be capped again or else taxed away. True, it is a lot of money, especially in a country where living standards are stagnating, and taxes are rising all the time. Venkat is making almost 250 times the median salary.

And yet, just for once, we should drop the class war, and all the anti-business rhetoric that comes with it, and recognise that he is worth every penny.

The award is completely justified. To start with, the bonus is tied to long-term performance. Barclays has had an exceptional year, with total profits for the last twelve months of £9.1bn, ahead of City estimates, and up from £8.1bn the year before.

The share price has risen by 58pc over the last year, reaching its highest level since before the financial crash, which is responsible for more than a third of the payout. Much of the improvement has been achieved by controlling costs at the investment banking unit, and concentrating instead on the business and consumer financial services in its core domestic market.

Perhaps most importantly it has been achieved at what has been a uniquely difficult time for all the traditional banks. No one should kid themselves that it has been easy to run any kind of financial institution these past few years. The high street banks have had to fend off the rise of app-based challengers such as Monzo and Revolut. The market has never been more brutally competitive than it has been for the last five years.

They have had to defend themselves against attempts by tech giants such as Apple and Amazon to muscle into finance as their next lucrative round of expansion. They have had to slim down their traditional branch networks, much of which has been rendered obsolete by technology, while keeping their customers on board and maintaining morale among staff. And they have had to support their small business customers at a time when stagnant growth and endless tax raids have pushed many of their core customers to the brink of bankruptcy.

Of course, it hasn’t all been perfect, and not everyone has been kept happy. Even so, Barclays has managed to tackle an exceptionally challenging few years while also increasing profits, and delivering exceptional returns for its shareholders,

Venkat is certainly more deserving of his pay than the men he succeeded. Jes Staley was quite rightly forced out the bank over his connections to Jeffrey Epstein, while Bob Diamond bet everything on trying to match the big players on Wall Street in investment banking, and, predictably, failed to deliver.

In Venkat, Barclays has finally found a modest, intelligent chief who has focused on its core British business and figured out how to make it work.

There is a lesson in that. Hard work can still deliver results if we allow it to be properly rewarded. And investment in the British economy can still deliver decent returns if it is done in the right way. It is hardly surprising that business confidence has hit record lows.

The Government has allowed spending to run out of control. It has hammered businesses with an endless series of tax rises, and it has failed to encourage investment. It has been the most anti-business, anti-enterprise administration Britain has had for at least half a century. Even so, the economy may be floundering, but it is not yet completely finished. There is still money to be made.

No doubt some of the Emilys and Torstens from the Resolution Foundation now in the engine room of the Treasury are already drawing up plans for a fresh windfall tax on the banks, for a higher rate of 60pc or more for anyone earning a million or more, and for an extra levy on share awards for bosses.

They might kid themselves that extra taxes will be popular with the voters, and keep the spending juggernaut rolling for a few more months before it finally collapses.

But instead of always condemning a successful British company, perhaps we should try celebrating one instead. Barclays has shown that hard work, focus, and investment can still pay off. We just need a few more major companies to follow that example – before it is too late.

data.title: ‘I made my fortune flipping properties, but I’ve had to adapt to stay rich’
data.description:
John Howard made £1m by “flipping” properties, trading houses for a profit after quick renovations – all by the age of 25.

The strategy was simple: spot an undervalued property, snap it up, rent it out, sell for a profit. Repeat. It worked a treat when house prices were soaring and borrowing was cheap.

Howard started out young, helping out at his father’s estate agency from the age of 11. In the following years, he took over the business as his father’s health failed.

“Around the time of my 18th birthday I was told by a developer about a couple of flats that were on the market,” he says. “I had a small amount of my own money and borrowed some cash from my mother and some from the bank to buy the properties for £9,800.” Before buying the flats, he’d already agreed to sell one of the tenants their home for £7,800.

This was an early lesson in spotting value, and the beginning of a lucrative career.

Instead of repeating this successful formula, Howard was constantly looking for new ways to make money from property. “I’ve tried to move on to another strategy before everyone else, and then when everyone else catches up, move to another one,” he says.

He later spotted an opportunity in regulated tenancies. The Rent Act of 1977 gave some tenants strong protections and rents were well below market value. Landlords would try to avoid these properties at all costs, and for many they were a nightmare. Tenants could stay for life if they wanted to, and the properties were traded at around half their market value as a result.

So Howard, now 64, would buy apartment blocks at these prices, and sell flats individually to tenants at 80pc of market value. Over time, some blocks became empty as tenants moved out or bought elsewhere. The vacant blocks could then be refurbished and sold at full market value.

Pursuing multiple strategies meant that he could adapt to a changing market environment. In the last decade, it has been much more difficult to simply “flip” a property by buying and selling without adding value.

A sluggish housing market means that spotting opportunities in valuations alone is rare. Prices are expected to grow just 2pc this year, according to estate agency Savills. And while after 2008, investors could rely on the ultra-low interest rates that fuelled house price growth, this came to a halt in 2022.

“The buy-to-let market has virtually disappeared,” says Howard. “That game has stopped because interest rates went up and up.” From 2015, landlords also had to pay more tax, while buy-to-let red tape built up.

So amid slumping price growth, fierce regulation and a spike in the cost of borrowing, Howard had to adapt – again. Developing properties can be much riskier, with high construction costs and time pressure, but the margins are far higher, and the opportunities are far greater.

In 2018, Howard acquired a failed development scheme on the Ipswich Waterfront for £1m. The bare towers were a symbol of decline, and for many, represented the 2008 financial crash. The original project had stalled since 2009 after its developers fell into administration. He secured government agency Homes England as a partner, which invested £26m, and the project was transformed into a luxury apartment complex, with Howard selling 150 flats.

Another strategy Howard has used is permitted development, where offices are converted into residential homes without the need for full planning permission. This has proved effective, but now his competitors are catching up to the trend, so he says he’s moving on.

Howard now owns 11 sites across the UK, each with eight to 85 homes. His property empire includes 48-bedroom hotel The Great White Horse and waterfront high-rise The Mill, both in Ipswich, where he lives. Last year, he sold 50 units and is developing 150, bringing the total properties sold to 4,500. 

There are other challenges, aside from the risk involved in property development. Costs have never been higher. Raw materials, labour and expensive loans weigh heavily on developers’ minds. For a lot of smaller housebuilders, it’s no longer worth the effort.

“I’ve got friends who have decided not to develop any more, as it’s not worth it. They’ve just had enough,” he says. One of their gripes is the UK’s planning system. Planning offices are under-resourced and are often the source of delays.

He adds: “A recent planning application has taken three and a half months just to get registered with the planning office because they’re so busy and can’t cope – it’s nonsense. How do Labour ever think they’re going to build 300,000 houses a year?”

So what can property entrepreneurs do? “Be mentally consistent every day, learn how to put a deal together, and finally, read the market,” he says.

There are still reasons to be hopeful, he says. Interest rates will continue to fall, and these savings will eventually be passed on. And although there are more difficulties in today’s market, there’s also more information available than ever, making it open to anyone.

“There’s probably 50 times as many people trying to do what I did back in the ’90s, so the competition is fierce,” adds Howard. “But having a multi-faceted approach is key. It means I can jump into different things. Don’t be a one-trick pony.”

data.title: Schroders to quit London stock market in £9.9bn sale to US rival
data.description:
Fund giant Schroders is to be sold to a US rival for £9.9bn, bringing its 67 years on the London stock market to an end.

The British money manager, which is one of the most recognised names in the City, has struck a merger deal with Nuveen, part of the Teachers Insurance and Annuity Association of America, a retirement savings group.

The proposed takeover will create one of the world’s largest asset managers with $2.5tn (£1.8tn) of assets, while ending Schroders’ independence after more than 200 years and dealing another blow to the ailing London Stock Exchange.

Richard Oldfield, Schroders’ chief executive, told The Telegraph the deal would “create the most powerful titan in active and wealth management globally”.

The deal is worth 612p per share, representing a 34pc premium to Schroders’ closing share price last night, which was 456p.

Dame Elizabeth Corley, Schroders’ chairman, insisted that despite quitting the stock market, London would “remain at the heart of this enlarged business”, serving as the combined group’s non-US headquarters.

Bosses also insisted that they did not intend to make any “material reductions” to the company’s headcount after the takeover, which is expected to close by the end of the year.

Mr Oldfield said, “If anyone thinks this is a step back from the UK or a step back from London, they haven’t really read the details of the deal.

“If we increase growth, London does even better than it would have done on a standalone basis.”

Schroders and Nuveen are expected to continue as standalone businesses.

Founded in 1804 by John Henry Schroder, the Schroders family still control around 44pc of the business, meaning they will make at least £4bn from the takeover.

Leonie Schroder and her cousin, Claire Fitzalan Howard, descendants of the founder, serve on the board.

Nuveen, founded in 1898 by John Nuveen, is headquartered in Chicago and currently manages $1.4tn in assets, with a heavy focus on the US market.

Mr Oldfield said the combination would “put us in the driving seat” in “an industry that is changing and consolidating quite rapidly”.

He added, “If we were on our own, we would be less able to do that.”

William Huffman, chief executive of Nuveen, added, “This transaction is about unlocking new growth opportunities for wealth and institutional investors around the world by giving our leading, differentiated public-to-private platform a broader global presence.”

While Schroders has been a fixture of the FTSE 100 for decades, its share price growth has stalled in recent years.

Last April, its valuation fell to its lowest point in a decade as its share price hit 302p.

Its struggles have largely stemmed from savers turning towards cheaper passive funds, rather than paying a premium for Schroder’s market expertise.

The money manager has struggled to move beyond its roots in stock picking as more ruthless US fund giants such as BlackRock and Blackstone have embraced profitable alternative markets, such as private credit.

Despite a mergers-and-acquisitions spree over the years, buying up smaller alternative managers focused on private capital, Schroders has struggled to turn its investments into shareholder returns.

Under Mr Oldfield, who was appointed in November 2024, Schroders has undertaken a steep cost-cutting programme, looking to shave £150m off its bottom line.

Schroders’ exit from the London Stock Exchange will represent a significant blow to the market, with the City stalwart joining a growing exodus of FTSE giants.

Nuveen said that if it chose to relist the business in the future, it would dual-list the company on the London market and another exchange.

The deal will be funded by cash and £3bn of debt.

data.title: Holiday home with stunning sea views on sale for £5k – buy it before it falls
data.description:
Marketed as a “short stroll” to the beach and boasting unrivalled sea views, a two-bed holiday home with a guide price of £5,000 seems like a steal.

But the imminent threat of sliding down the cliff edge means the coastal retreat comes with a significantly limited lifespan.

The bungalow, in Freshwater, on the western side of the Isle of Wight, sits precariously on the edge of one of the fastest-eroding stretches of coastline in Britain.

Two neighbouring properties were condemned and demolished in 2024 after being deemed unsafe for habitation.

Despite the looming threat – made even more apparent by recent landfalls caused by this year’s wet weather – the owners are hoping to sell the home.

It will be going under the hammer via the “modern method of auction” (MMoA), which involves a longer bidding process.

Pittis, the estate agency, has marketed the property as “a rare opportunity for a coastal holiday retreat with unbeatable scenery at a jaw-dropping price”. The holiday home commands sea views of Hurst Castle and Colwell Bay.

It said those interested in bidding were advised to view the property as the “price reflects the proximity to the cliff edge”.

Emma Carter, of Freshwater parish council, told The Telegraph: “There’s always been coastal erosion there but it’s certainly been quite considerable in the past three years.

“The wet weather meant we had some slips last week and in the past there have been considerable chunks that have come down.

“I wouldn’t be comfortable standing on the edge by the building and being that close to the cliff without thinking that it’s windy enough to blow me over.”

The bungalow used to sit close to an old concrete gun emplacement which was built into the cliff, but this recently succumbed to erosion. The cliffs are primarily composed of soft silt and clay, making them susceptible to rapid erosion.

Should the house follow the same fate as the gun emplacement, responsibility for clearing up the debris typically lies with the property owner.

In promoting their listing on social media, Pittis described the home as a “steal”.

It cannot be used as a full-time residence and is limited to 41 weeks of occupancy per year. Due to this, it escapes the second homes council tax premium, but the property does attract undisclosed site fees.

It has a guide price of £5,000, yet comes with a minimum reservation fee of £6,000. This non-refundable fee triggers a 56-day window for the transaction to go through.

The buyer also has to pay £349 towards the preparation cost of the sales information pack.

‘Does it come with a parachute?’

The listing has divided locals on social media.

One warned: “Have a look at recent cliff falls combined with all the floods they have had this weekend. You might get one very expensive holiday before it vanishes.”

Another asked: “Does it come with a parachute?”

Estate agents selling properties in other areas at risk of erosion have been criticised for their cut-price deals.

Homes in Hemsby, Norfolk, have been put on the market for as little as £30,000 in recent months amid a swathe of demolitions in the area due to erosion.

In its guidance, National Trading Standards stipulates that a property listing “should include, if relevant, an accurate description or statement as to any known risk of coastal erosion affecting the property and its boundary”.

Ultimately, the onus falls on the surveyor and the buyer to identify and gauge the erosion risks at a seaside property.

The bungalow in Freshwater has undergone recent renovations, with the kitchen and bathroom both being modernised.

Pittis was approached for comment.
