data.title: One mad decision shows just how broken our planning system is
data.description:
Even some of the world’s largest construction projects in Asia and the Middle East have faced opposition from locals.

Take “The Line” in Saudi Arabia, a 100-mile-long city that forms part of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s vast Neom project. He has described it as a “civilisational revolution”, which is somewhat at odds with the forced eviction of an estimated 20,000 members of the al-Huwaitat tribe to build it.

In China, the Three Gorges hydroelectric gravity dam on the Yangtze River is regarded by the Chinese regime as a monumental engineering marvel. But it too has faced protests from some of the 1.2 million people it displaced.

Environmentalists are angry about the destruction of wildlife, including the giant Chinese paddlefish, which is now believed to be extinct due to the dam’s construction.

In the UK, High Speed 2 (HS2), Heathrow’s third runway, Sizewell C and the Stonehenge Tunnel have all attracted the ire of Nimbys and environmentalists.

The concerns around some schemes are understandable. If there is sizeable opposition – particularly from locals – then they may deserve to be scuppered.

My own view on HS2 was that it is a vast sum of money to spend on something with only negligible benefits – funds that would be much better spent elsewhere.

But far too often in the UK, the dead hand of bureaucracy threatens to stand in the way of progress.

Public trust in the British planning system is already desperately low.

Many people rightly view it as ineffective and overly complex. In 2019, a YouGov survey found just 7pc trust the planning system.

And for every mega-project that gets blocked, there are hundreds of other much smaller but still significant developments that fail to get past the post.

Step forward those who recommended that Hackney Council block the Shoreditch Works development, though not before developers had spent 18 months trying to navigate a bewildering maze of red tape.

If you distrust the planning system, the Shoreditch debacle could give you an aneurysm.

The proposals submitted to the Labour-led Hackney council had received emphatic support from locals. A firm of consultants hired by the developers presented more than 2,000 people with three pairs of “before” and “after” images of the area from identical angles.

Support for these new developments ranged from 76pc to 78pc of the respondents.

The resulting report said backing “held firm across every possible demographic of socio-economic status, politics, age, race, region or sex”. And it’s easy to see why.

The scheme would deliver 78 new homes, 35pc of which would be affordable, in an area that has become one of the most gentrified in London.

It would also add 80,500 sq meters of new commercial, lab and office space right next to the City, while creating 500 jobs and apprenticeships.

Despite the myriad benefits, planning officials appear to have gone out of their way to object.

Much of this appears to be on spurious grounds. One wonders if they had made up their minds before the proposals were submitted.

An analysis of the planning report by Britain Remade, a think tank, found that developers submitted 9,084 pages of paperwork.

As one keen-eyed observer noted on social media, that compares to 3,400 pages for all seven Harry Potter books put together and roughly 1,200 pages for the first edition of War and Peace.

Yet planners said the proposals were not detailed enough.

This is despite developers being asked to comply with 42 Hackney planning policies, 75 separate policies for Sir Sadiq Khan’s “London Plan” to tackle the capital’s housing crisis, as well as numerous other bits of national and local legislation and guidance.

Contrast this with the planning applications Britain Remade looked at from the 1930s, which were often a few pages long at most and took just weeks to secure approval.

The objections raised are no less farcical than the infuriating system that developers have been forced to endure.

Planners argued there weren’t enough homes and that the scheme didn’t provide enough office space.

Yet with the scheme also dismissed for being too tall – in an area overlooked by many of the tallest buildings in Britain, no less – how the developers address those particular issues is anyone’s guess.

Another criticism from the Design Review Panel was that every proposed building was of the same architectural style, and they wanted something more eclectic, as if housing schemes should cater to the personal design whims of a handful of professional planners.

Never mind what local residents think.

Reports suggest the council received just three letters of opposition.

The simple truth is that rejecting plans on the basis that they don’t provide enough homes results in no houses being built at all.

The list of genuinely bizarre objections from the planning officers to what is clearly a well-thought-out mixed-use housing development at a time when Britain is well behind on its house-building targets goes on and on.

Even more worrying, the construction industry says this is happening across the country multiple times a day.

Yet ministers think it is a good idea to delegate more decisions to planning officers, not less.

One can only hope Hackney councillors reject the recommendation and vote in favour. It would represent a victory for common sense and for progress, too.

Sir Keir Starmer claims the Government’s “growth mission” will result in higher productivity and living standards, but unless something is done about this country’s bureaucratic swamp, Britain is doomed to decades of stagnation.

data.title: AI mania is now spilling into space
data.description:
How do you put a value on a city on the Moon? What about a colony on Mars? Or an AI superintelligence zooming through space aboard a million satellites?

If SpaceX’s bankers are to be believed, this futuristic vision should be worth at least $1.5tn (£1.1tn) at an upcoming initial public offering for Elon Musk’s rocket business.

This week, Musk promised SpaceX would aim to complete a self-sustaining lunar city within 10 years and start work on a Mars colony within the next five.

Even next to Tesla, SpaceX, founded in 2002, is Musk’s crowning glory. The business has a near-monopoly over the West’s space launch market and its Starlink network is vital to America’s national security. Its Starship rockets should enable humans to return to the Moon. This year, it is expected to hit $24bn in revenues.

SpaceX is now rumoured to be seeking to raise $50bn at a valuation of $1.5tn in its planned stock market listing.

Yet despite the company’s out-of-this-world origins, its valuation and future success are now increasingly tied to the AI mania gripping the stock market.

In December, Musk began to float the idea that SpaceX could build a fleet of AI data centres in orbit. The world’s richest man wants to build a “sentient sun” to challenge OpenAI’s ChatGPT.

To that end, Musk has merged SpaceX with xAI, the company that owns his Grok chatbot and X social network. SpaceX’s board valued his rocket business at $1tn as part of the deal. xAI, which lost nearly $8bn last year, was valued at $250bn.

If it secures a valuation of $1.5tn in June when it is slated to go public, SpaceX’s value will have more than tripled in a year from $400bn last summer.

Musk would tell you that the reason for the heady increase is the huge potential offered by AI data centres in space.

In orbit, data centres would have access to always-on power from the Sun and avoid the planning and regulatory pitfalls that accompany carpeting large parts of Earth in solar panels. The power from these data centres would help develop and run a future AI superintelligence, benefitting SpaceX’s new division xAI.

That, at least, is the theory.

The past year has seen a boom in the valuations of big tech stocks and private AI labs, such as OpenAI and Anthropic, allowing them to promise a multitrillion-dollar spending spree on AI data centres.

This has also lifted a handful of space companies seen as potential alternatives to Musk’s SpaceX, which could feasibly send their own data centres into orbit.

The VanEck Space ETF (trading under the ticker JEDI) has doubled in value over the last 12 months. AST Space Mobile, a space business developing mobile broadband satellites, has surged 230pc in a year and is worth $36bn. It has forecast revenues of just $685m this year, up from about $18m a year earlier. Rocket Lab, a US launch business, is now worth about $40bn, up 150pc over the same period.

Planet Labs, an Earth imaging business, has seen its market value climb 270pc. In November, it emerged that it was working with Google on the internet search giant’s own programme to put AI data centres into orbit. “The enthusiasm around Elon Musk is fuelling the interest in those companies,” says Pierre Lionnet, an economist at Eurospace.

All of this raises the prospect that a potential bubble in AI stocks is now fuelling an emerging space bubble.

For some investors, Musk’s decision to merge SpaceX and xAI is a logical move. He can rely on SpaceX’s access to cheap rocket launches on Falcon 9, and in future on his Starship mega rocket, to blast ahead of AI companies on Earth.

Mark Boggett, of UK space investor Seraphim, says: “We completely agree with the thesis that AI will not scale on Earth. It will scale in orbit.”

Jodi Bartin, managing director of advisory firm Citicourt & Co and founder of investment fund Space4Earth, adds that the deal offers Musk “a huge advantage in both the highly competitive AI race”.

She also notes that the deal would “certainly set the stage for institutional investing into the space sector” as major funds flock to the SpaceX float.

Some of Musk’s most vocal backers have argued the SpaceX float is just the start. Analysts at Ark Invest have claimed that Musk’s company could be worth $2.5tn by 2030.

Yet not everyone is convinced.

The “sudden promotion” of AI data centres appears to be an attempt to “ride the current AI investment wave”, says Christian Baron von der Ropp, a space consultant. The deal enables investors to “cash out at unsustainable valuations just before reality kicks in”, he believes.

At its proposed valuation, SpaceX would be worth more than the top six biggest US defence and space giants combined. It would also be worth more than OpenAI, run by Musk’s bitter rival Sam Altman, which is currently worth about $500bn.

SpaceX has “substantial advantages” when it comes to launching a network of space data centres, thanks to its expertise with Starlink and its access to cheap launch, Tim Farrar, a space analyst at TMF Associates, wrote in a recent blog post.

That, however, is very different to saying the market can “justify a $1.5tn valuation”.

Farrar tells The Telegraph: “The valuation can only be justified based on a belief that Elon Musk will come up with huge new business opportunities, whether that’s orbital data centres or Moon tourism.

“That’s no different to any other Musk business. You can’t justify Tesla’s valuation based on the cars it’s building.”

Musk has certainly outdone himself with his promises for SpaceX’s future. In a blog post this month, he claimed the company would launch one million satellites, taking a step towards becoming a so-called “Kardashev II” civilisation – or an interstellar species that can harvest the entire energy of the Sun.

On X, Musk said the space industry “will vastly exceed the value of all of Earth”, harnessing “100,000 times more energy than Earth” by using solar energy from space.

However, there are also substantial uncertainties over the prospects of data centres in space, both from a technical standpoint and whether they would ever be economically viable.

Ultimately, the xAI deal links Musk’s successful rocket business to a much more speculative AI bet. And while mania about AI has powered US stock markets to ever greater heights, recent volatility shows growing investor nerves about whether the huge investments planned will ultimately pay off.

“I don’t think this is a good thing for the original SpaceX investors,” says Lionnet. “And these guys have been waiting almost 20 years.”

Still, Lionnet expects there will be plenty of demand from investors when SpaceX finally does kick off its float.

“The fundamentals will not affect the valuation for the IPO,” he says. “It is not rational, but Elon Musk fans do not care.”

data.title: Daily Mail owner’s Telegraph takeover faces four-month investigation
data.description:
The proposed takeover of The Telegraph by Lord Rothermere, the Daily Mail owner, will face a four-month initial investigation by regulators over fears it would hand him too much sway over the press.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, confirmed that the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) and Ofcom would have until June 10 to deliver their preliminary assessments of the £500m deal.

The 16-week schedule is double the statutory requirement of 40 working days. The referral comes more than three weeks after Ms Nandy said she was minded to involve regulators.

The CMA and Ofcom will examine whether the combination of The Telegraph with Lord Rothermere’s existing news portfolio threatens the public interests in a plurality of ownership and a plurality of views. As well as the Mail titles online and in print, he owns the freesheet Metro and the i.

Lord Rothermere has said he plans to invest in The Telegraph’s international growth and backed its editorial leadership. He has argued that the explosion of news sources online means there should be no plurality concerns over a merger.

But in letters to DMGT, Lord Rothermere’s holding company, and to the independent directors currently overseeing the Telegraph, Ms Nandy’s officials said that the digital reach of the proposed combination was grounds for concern. DMGT titles were accessed by 53pc of UK internet users and The Telegraph by 37pc in July last year. They also warned that print remained influential.

The letters said: “This provides grounds to be concerned that the merger has the potential to result in the loss of a significant and distinct voice in the online news market and a concentration of control.”

The officials appeared to clarify an earlier stance from Ms Nandy that there was an “acute” risk of loss of plurality “especially in the UK’s Right-leaning market”, which it is understood to have sparked protests from DMGT that it potentially prejudiced investigations. The latest letter from Ms Nandy’s officials said any political definition of the markets under scrutiny was a matter for regulators.

If regulators identify a threat to the public interest in their initial investigation it is possible that Lord Rothermere could offer concessions, such as legally binding undertakings on the editorial independence of The Telegraph from the Mail titles, or the sale of Metro and the i.

That could avoid a full “phase two” investigation by the CMA, which could result in the takeover being blocked and heavy costs for DMGT and potentially RedBird IMI, the seller. That could take an additional six months and, with administrative interludes, extend the uncertainty into 2027.

RedBird IMI is a collaboration between the United Arab Emirates and the US private equity firm RedBird Capital. Its own attempt to gain control of The Telegraph was blocked nearly two years ago following a cross-party outcry and legislation banning foreign state ownership.

It has since made repeated faltering attempts to recoup its £500m investment in an onward sale at a price which few investment bankers, media executives or analysts can justify.

However, Lord Rothermere has made two previous attempts to buy The Telegraph and DMGT believes it is uniquely positioned to extract cost savings from back office and commercial functions that will underpin its investment.

The initial “phase one” investigation of Lord Rothermere’s bid may not end until after the three-year anniversary of the beginning of The Telegraph’s ownership saga. In early June 2023, Lloyds Banking Group seized control of the company from the Barclay family over their unpaid debt of £1.2bn.

RedBird IMI and the UAE derailed a planned auction by paying off the debt in a complex transaction intended to deliver them control of The Telegraph. Instead, Redbird IMI has been barred from exercising influence despite having its money tied up in the company.

The leisurely regulatory schedule appears to signal a change of posture from the Government. In November Ms Nandy said her “particular concerns are that the protracted uncertainty has been detrimental to the stability of the Telegraph and its staff” and spoke of the “need for the period of uncertainty to be ended”.

Lord Rothermere’s proposed takeover is the fifth attempt to secure new ownership and now faces significant additional uncertainty.

The political and market backdrop now includes attempted Labour leadership coups from the Left and a renewed focus on press regulation, as well as a steep downward swing in the valuations of companies viewed as exposed to AI.

The takeover is to be financed by a £400m loan from NatWest and a £100m deferred payment to RedBird IMI, due within two years. Credit ratings agencies have warned that DMGT faces a downgrade as a result of the transaction and the greater exposure to the digital challenges faced by news publishers, which could increase its cost of borrowing.

Despite intervening in the proposed takeover, Ms Nandy has not yet given RedBird IMI permission to transfer ownership of the option it holds over The Telegraph to DMGT, meaning no money has yet changed hands.

Ms Nandy’s officials said she was “keenly aware of the financial difficulties facing the newspaper industry”.

DMGT was contacted for comment.

data.title: Trump’s competition chief accuses Apple News of Left-wing bias
data.description:
Donald Trump’s competition chief has accused Apple of “systematically” promoting Left-wing news articles in its iPhone news app and burying Right-leaning articles.

Andrew Ferguson, the chairman of the US Federal Trade Commission, wrote to Tim Cook, Apple’s chief executive, demanding the tech giant take action over claims it has “suppressed” conservative news stories for “political reasons” in the Apple News app.

Mr Ferguson wrote: “Multiple studies have found that in recent months Apple News has chosen not to feature a single article from an American conservative-leaning news source, while simultaneously promoting hundreds of articles from liberal publications.”

The FTC chief, who was nominated by Mr Trump, highlighted a study by a conservative think tank in the US, which claimed Apple had failed to include a single story from Fox News or the New York Post in its top news section when it was checked once per day in January.

The study claimed Apple News favoured promoting posts from the Washington Post, which has been critical of the Trump administration.

Apple News, the iPhone’s news aggregation app, is curated by human editors as well as a digital algorithm. The app can be customised by users to follow different news sources, including those which lean Right or Left. However, some hard-Right US news sites, including Breitbart News, are not available through Apple News.

In September, The Telegraph revealed that the Daily Mail had accused Apple of “arbitrary and discriminatory practices” by blocking it from joining its Apple News service in the UK.

The Daily Mail was already part of Apple News in the US, but had not initially joined the UK version of its service when it launched.

The Daily Mail said in a submission to the Competition and Markets Authority: “Not being a participant in Apple News is no longer an option – it has become indispensable to running a successful online news publishing business.”

In January, Apple reversed course and the Daily Mail was added to its news app in the UK.

AllSides, a US business that rates the political bias of news sources, has claimed Apple News is marginally Left-leaning.

Mr Ferguson warned Apple could be breaking FTC rules if it was found to “suppress or promote news articles based on the perceived ideological or political viewpoint of the article or publication”.

Republican officials repeatedly accused Silicon Valley of Left-wing bias and censorship during the administration of Democratic president Joe Biden.

Last summer, the FTC alleged Google had employed “partisan” spam filtering in its Gmail app that blocked Republican fundraising emails. Google denied the claim.

Mr Trump has taken aim at Apple over its reliance on China for its iPhone production, repeatedly threatening the company with tariffs.

The tech giant has sought to mollify the iPhone maker by promising to invest $600bn (£440bn) in the US, including bolstering domestic manufacturing.

Apple was contacted for comment.

data.title: Daily Mail owner’s Telegraph takeover faces four-month investigation
data.description:
The proposed takeover of The Telegraph by Lord Rothermere, the Daily Mail owner, will face a four-month initial investigation by regulators over fears it would hand him too much sway over the press.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, confirmed that the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) and Ofcom would have until June 10 to deliver their preliminary assessments of the £500m deal.

The 16-week schedule is double the statutory requirement of 40 working days. The referral comes more than three weeks after Ms Nandy said she was minded to involve regulators.

The CMA and Ofcom will examine whether the combination of The Telegraph with Lord Rothermere’s existing news portfolio threatens the public interests in a plurality of ownership and a plurality of views. As well as the Mail titles online and in print, he owns the freesheet Metro and the i.

Lord Rothermere has said he plans to invest in The Telegraph’s international growth and backed its editorial leadership. He has argued that the explosion of news sources online means there should be no plurality concerns over a merger.

But in letters to DMGT, Lord Rothermere’s holding company, and to the independent directors currently overseeing the Telegraph, Ms Nandy’s officials said that the digital reach of the proposed combination was grounds for concern. DMGT titles were accessed by 53pc of UK internet users and The Telegraph by 37pc in July last year. They also warned that print remained influential.

The letters said: “This provides grounds to be concerned that the merger has the potential to result in the loss of a significant and distinct voice in the online news market and a concentration of control.”

The officials appeared to clarify an earlier stance from Ms Nandy that there was an “acute” risk of loss of plurality “especially in the UK’s Right-leaning market”, which it is understood to have sparked protests from DMGT that it potentially prejudiced investigations. The latest letter from Ms Nandy’s officials said any political definition of the markets under scrutiny was a matter for regulators.

If regulators identify a threat to the public interest in their initial investigation it is possible that Lord Rothermere could offer concessions, such as legally binding undertakings on the editorial independence of The Telegraph from the Mail titles, or the sale of Metro and the i.

That could avoid a full “phase two” investigation by the CMA, which could result in the takeover being blocked and heavy costs for DMGT and potentially RedBird IMI, the seller. That could take an additional six months and, with administrative interludes, extend the uncertainty into 2027.

RedBird IMI is a collaboration between the United Arab Emirates and the US private equity firm RedBird Capital. Its own attempt to gain control of The Telegraph was blocked nearly two years ago following a cross-party outcry and legislation banning foreign state ownership.

It has since made repeated faltering attempts to recoup its £500m investment in an onward sale at a price which few investment bankers, media executives or analysts can justify.

However, Lord Rothermere has made two previous attempts to buy The Telegraph and DMGT believes it is uniquely positioned to extract cost savings from back office and commercial functions that will underpin its investment.

The initial “phase one” investigation of Lord Rothermere’s bid may not end until after the three-year anniversary of the beginning of The Telegraph’s ownership saga. In early June 2023, Lloyds Banking Group seized control of the company from the Barclay family over their unpaid debt of £1.2bn.

RedBird IMI and the UAE derailed a planned auction by paying off the debt in a complex transaction intended to deliver them control of The Telegraph. Instead, Redbird IMI has been barred from exercising influence despite having its money tied up in the company.

The leisurely regulatory schedule appears to signal a change of posture from the Government. In November Ms Nandy said her “particular concerns are that the protracted uncertainty has been detrimental to the stability of the Telegraph and its staff” and spoke of the “need for the period of uncertainty to be ended”.

Lord Rothermere’s proposed takeover is the fifth attempt to secure new ownership and now faces significant additional uncertainty.

The political and market backdrop now includes attempted Labour leadership coups from the Left and a renewed focus on press regulation, as well as a steep downward swing in the valuations of companies viewed as exposed to AI.

The takeover is to be financed by a £400m loan from NatWest and a £100m deferred payment to RedBird IMI, due within two years. Credit ratings agencies have warned that DMGT faces a downgrade as a result of the transaction and the greater exposure to the digital challenges faced by news publishers, which could increase its cost of borrowing.

Despite intervening in the proposed takeover, Ms Nandy has not yet given RedBird IMI permission to transfer ownership of the option it holds over The Telegraph to DMGT, meaning no money has yet changed hands.

Ms Nandy’s officials said she was “keenly aware of the financial difficulties facing the newspaper industry”.

DMGT was contacted for comment.

data.title: Eco-homes backed by King left to decay
data.description:
A hamlet of eco-homes backed by the King has been left in decay 13 years after the properties were built.

The seven mock-Georgian homes have stood abandoned since they were built in 2013 at Coed Darcy, a £1.2bn development on the site of a former oil refinery near Port Talbot, South Wales.

Plans for the 4,000-home scheme were inspired by the King’s model village, Poundbury, where residents are encouraged to live and work in the same community, and it was backed by his Prince’s Foundation for Building Community, which championed sustainable building.

However, only 294 homes have been built to date after its initial developer became mired in financial difficulties, with residents complaining that it has no schools, shops or doctors’ surgeries.

On the western edge of the 1,060-acre development stand seven Georgian-style properties overlooking fields, wetlands and woodland.

Built in 2013 to test building methods that were set to be used elsewhere in the village, they were complete with gardens, lamp posts and sash windows.

But now they stand discoloured and partially boarded up, with a mile of wasteland separating them from the other homes built in the village.

As recently as 2019, St Modwen, the initial developer of the site, claimed the hamlet was “sat there ready to be occupied”.

But the homes have never been occupied and were recently compared by a film-maker to “something out of an apocalyptic movie”.

“It just looked completely abandoned,” said Jay Curtis, 38, who filmed the site using a drone.

In 2008, plans were drawn up for the development that included 4,000 homes for 10,000 residents and four schools.

The thousands of “sustainable” homes were set to be built with traditional Welsh stone, and would repurpose the brownfield land vacated by a former BP oil refinery.

Neath Port Talbot council described the scheme at the time as establishing “a sustainable community of 4,000 traditionally styled Welsh homes”.

When building work began in 2013, the King visited the village, the buildings of which were designed by Ben Pentreath, who is responsible for much of the development at Poundbury.

“In a number of years, the site will be incorporated into the wider master plan and the houses will be occupied,” Mr Pentreath said at the time. But building work soon stalled because of “financial difficulties”.

In 2023, Alun Llewelyn, a Plaid Cymru councillor on Neath Port Talbot council, said it had faced “major challenges”, including the 2008 financial crisis.

St Modwen has since become STM Brighton Group and is now managed by Revantage.

The company submitted a scaled-back planning application for 1,800 new homes in 2021 which excluded the site of the hamlet.

Rob Williams, its then director of major projects, said the new village would be inspired by the “15-minute neighbourhood”, a controversial planning concept which holds that a person should be able to access amenities within a quarter of an hour’s walk or cycle without needing to drive.

“The pandemic has changed the way people think about their living space, community areas, and the importance of work-life balance,” he said in 2022.

“Our new master plan focuses on the principles of creating a ‘15-minute neighbourhood’, with the community able to access many daily needs within a short distance of their homes.

“While the scheme has evolved from the original planning consent, our vision for Coed Darcy remains just as ambitious and just as innovative. It will be more relevant and deliverable for the needs of the area, more sustainable, and aspires to be an exemplar for Wales.”

The proposals have not yet been approved.

Mr Curtis said there was “no real, clear answer” about why the development had floundered.

“There’s no real, clear answer,” he said. “That’s what baffles most people. To have that level of hype, a royal visit, and such ambition – and then to see it all just left – it amazes people.

“These are big, expensive homes. There are a lot of them. And no one ever moved in.”

A spokesman for STM Brighton Group said: “We have sought to engage with the council on scheme viability for a number of years and submitted revised proposals last year. We are now working closely with the council and progress is dependent on a planning decision, which we hope will be delivered imminently so the site can be taken forward without further unnecessary delay.”

A spokesman for Neath Port Talbot council said: “The development at Coed Darcy has been the subject of ongoing discussions over many years with previous developers St Modwen and now its parent company Blackstone Group, which, after purchasing St Modwen, moved the ownership and responsibility of the site to a new company, Revantage.

“The development has largely stalled in recent years in a difficult economic climate with previous applications having sought to vary the terms of the legal agreement relating to the overall development to make such development viable and deliverable.”

data.title: The French town that puts Britain’s new homes to shame
data.description:
Before she moved to Clamart, Elena Harty had never had much pride in anywhere she had lived.

London had felt too busy and dangerous; Paris too impersonal, its old apartments too poky and temperamental.

But the new town of Clamart Panorama, a former brownfield site just 8km southwest of the French capital, ticks all the boxes.

It has schools, parks, restaurants, a wine bar and a boulangerie all within a 10-minute walk. There are clubs and societies for all age groups; lush green spaces for joggers and dog walkers. It has an abundance of flowers, a network of cycle routes and a direct tram line into Paris.

“We’ve even got a heron,” says Harty, who is 33 and moved from Britain after meeting her partner.

The couple were initially drawn to the area in 2021 by its proximity to the capital and its spacious mid-rise apartments, which imitate the ornate 19th-century mansion blocks that line Paris’s grand boulevards.

In the years since, they have watched Clamart grow into a thriving town of some 5,000 residents. “There are people from really all walks of life,” says Harty.

Affordable housing accounts for 30pc of apartments, but they are mixed in the same blocks as private dwellings, which has given rise to a diverse and close-knit community.

It stands in stark contrast to recent projects in Britain, which have faced criticism for creating drab, low-quality and characterless “ghost towns” full of houses but lacking community and social spaces.

Clamart florist Marie Paolini says her clientele are mainly young professionals and families who have moved from Paris in search of more space. “It’s a really dynamic place,” she says. “We are right next to the tram too, so we get a lot of customers.”

During the day, locals stop and chat between trips to the café and cheese shop, while at night the tables at Bistrot Papa Pool and pizzeria La Bella Vita buzz with hungry diners.

Adrien Lorente, who runs Clamart wine bar La Cave Lelay, says it has started to become difficult to get a table without booking ahead. “We are always full,” he says. “I work from 3pm until midnight and we’re full every time.”

Construction of Clamart Panorama began in 2017 as part of plans to relieve pressure on Paris’s housing market by creating new commutable suburbs for workers.

Work is still underway, with completion of its third and final phase expected for early 2027. But it has already started to become a blueprint for new towns across France.

Britons unconvinced on new-builds

It is a far cry from the latest new-build efforts in Britain. Often derided for being soulless, ugly and lacking a civic heart, the UK’s recent developments have struggled to win public approval.

A 2023 survey by the Chartered Institute of Building found that 40pc of Britons think new-build housing lacks character, while a third considers it poor quality.

New towns built in the last 10 years such as Ebbsfleet, in Kent, and Sherford, in Devon, have faced considerable backlash after homes flew up without any of the most basic amenities such as shops and GP surgeries.

The 3,000-home new town Cranbrook, in Devon, earned the nickname “Crimebrook” after an alleged rise in anti-social behaviour among bored teenagers. But other new developments have simply been accused of being eerily quiet or “dystopian”.

It is a concerning trend as Labour pushes forward with plans to build 12 new towns across Britain, as part of its pledge to build 1.5 million homes by 2029.

Ike Ijeh, of think tank Policy Exchange, says problems in Britain lie in its dogmatic approach to density.

Whereas mixed-used apartment blocks of between four and eight storeys are a common feature of European towns and cities, British developers have tended to prioritise low-rise suburban developments. Nearly 80pc of homes across England are a house or a bungalow, according to the latest census data.

“We idealise culturally in this country the idea of a house – far more than the idea of a flat,” he says. “Developers are aware of the old adage: an Englishman’s home is his castle. That really differentiates us from the Continent.”

But low-rise sprawl carries problems for both developers and the people who live there.

Financially, it is an inefficient use of space. For a similar amount of land required for a two-storey house, a developer could build a mid-rise block containing dozens of apartments and sell them off at more affordable prices.

It also encourages a homes-first, amenities-later approach that often leaves the construction of retail and hospitality spaces delayed until certain population thresholds are met. This means early arrivals can find themselves effectively living in a “ghost town” where they are surrounded by nothing but other houses.

In Clamart, on the other hand, shops, restaurants and local services occupy street-level spaces in many of the residential buildings. This is something Guy Courtois, head of urbanist think tank Pour une Renaissance Urbaine, says is crucial to its success.

“Single houses wouldn’t work. You need density,” he says. “If you want to have a lively town you need to have shops at a floor level, beautiful architecture, parks and greenery. We have all those things here.”

Where mid-rise blocks dominate, easy access to nature is fundamental. In Clamart, balconied apartments are turned towards an expansive central lake, which helps provide a constant sense of the outdoors.

Ijeh says that because mid-rise buildings achieve higher density using less space, developers tend to be able to build in more green space.

“Mid-rise is so efficient at providing density it means there’s more land available for landscaping, compared to what you would have in a low-rise and to a degree in high-rise scenario where you need lots of areas around tall buildings,” he says.

The tram, which takes just 20 minutes to travel into Paris, also plays a crucial role in making the area attractive to investors.

“Bus routes can be changed or cancelled,” says Eleanor Broad, at Create Streets think tank. “Whereas a tram line gives developers confidence about investing in the area.”

‘The key for success is a good vision from a mayor’

Clamart Panorama was not the first development in France to adopt new standards of urban planning.

Just a few kilometres down the road is Le-Plessis Robinson, a much larger town of 30,000, which laid the foundations for Clamart back in the late 1980s.

Swathes of the town’s post-war concrete tower blocks were pulled down and replaced with buildings in the neo-traditional style after new mayor Philippe Pemezec pledged to reverse the area’s decline.

Putting beauty and community at the centre of the project, Pemezec’s vision was a success. Its population rebounded, retail vacancy rates improved and visitors from around the world flocked to see the town’s transformation.

However, like Clamart, it hinged on a factor distinct to France: the authority of city mayors.

In Britain, the majority of urban planning decisions are made by elected planning committees. In France, the responsibility falls to one of the country’s more than 34,000 local mayors, who wield significant influence and control over developments in their commune.

“The number one factor for success is a good vision from a mayor, because they have the power to block any project,” says Courtois.

This means city halls can acquire land at relatively low prices, helping to keep down the overall cost of projects and support the execution of more ambitious ideas.

“Otherwise the mayor can just say, ‘If you don’t want to sell it to me, you’ll do nothing with the land – because I will block anything else’,” says Courtois.

When the city hall bought the land to build Clamart Panorama, it was a brownfield site owned by energy giant EDF. The city hall paid €50m and auctioned off lots to developers for a total of €100m. This gave them €50m to reinvest back into local services.

Watering down the original masterplan is also a lot more difficult than in Britain, where arguing about the cost or speed of construction can often persuade local authorities to loosen design codes. In France, a mayor with a strong vision can simply tell the developer that unless they follow the masterplan to the letter, they will have to sell their lot to a company that can see it through.

Courtois estimates that less than 1pc of new-build projects in France adhere to the neo-traditional principles of Clamart or Le Plessis-Robinson, despite costing just 10 to 15pc more to construct than modern developments.

But the developments are not short of detractors. Neo-traditional architectural styles continue to be dogged by accusations of being pastiche and of creating “toy towns”, and Clamart is not immune to such criticism.

Although the town has a similar look to the 19th-century buildings in Paris, some say the modern methods of using concrete frames and reconstituted stone facades appear less sturdy than Baron Haussmann’s load-bearing limestone structures. The materials also tend to weather differently to real stone, which can make buildings look shabbier over time, rather than adding character.

However, Courtois believes such criticism is largely immaterial, particularly when compared to the towns that post-war modernism provided. “Ask the people who live here,” he says. “If they are happy, then it’s a success.”

data.title: Driverless car makes emergency stop after being fooled by advert on bus
data.description:
A driverless car mistook an advertisement on the side of a bus for a group of real people and performed an emergency stop, it has been revealed.

The poster – which was promoting the 2015 spy film The Man from U.N.C.L.E. – featured several actors who were identified by the vehicle’s AI system as pedestrians in the road.

John McDermid, a professor of software engineering at the University of York, revealed details of the incident during a briefing in London to discuss the technology.

This week, Waymo, the US driverless car firm, announced that it would launch a pilot of its robotaxi service in London this year, while Wayve is expected to roll out the service with Uber later in the year.

However, British experts cautioned that the technology was not perfect. They said that during trials, driverless cars struggled to distinguish between real people and images, understand roundabouts, identify rule-breaking by pedestrians, and know when to slow down on a tight curve.

During the briefing at the Science Media Centre, Prof McDermid said: “One of the automated vehicle companies I work with had a situation where their vehicle did a sudden emergency stop because it’s all pedestrians in the road, except they weren’t.

“It was a life-size advert on the side of a bus, but to an AI, it was human beings. That seems very obvious [to us], but actually, to the AI, it’s not.”

Prof McDermid also showed a video of automated car trials in York. He said the vehicles struggled when people made unexpected movements, such as crossing a road while the pedestrian light was red.

He said: “It’s seen that there’s a traffic light, so identified... the hazard, because the light is red. It changes to green, the vehicle is about to move off. But this is York, so the tourists, although the lights change to green, they still walk across the road.

“Computer vision doesn’t understand what it doesn’t have models for in the world; it doesn’t know what a roundabout is.”

However, experts said that automation offered an opportunity to address some of the factors that contribute to road casualties.

More than 1,600 people die on British roads annually, with road crashes one of the most common causes of death among young people.

Under the Automated Vehicles Act, passed in 2024, self-driving vehicles on British roads must achieve a level of safety equivalent to, or higher than, that of careful and competent human drivers.

But researchers said that while humans were very good at recognising the sanctity of life, and largely drove carefully to avoid harm to themselves and others, computer systems did not have such a moral code.

Dr Nick Reed, an independent expert consultant at Reed Mobility, said there was something “fundamentally different” between how AI systems viewed people and how humans did.

He said: “[Humans] understand that there are personal consequences if they get caught breaking the rules.

“These behaviours are inherently human and are based on millions of years of evolution. We can’t have the same confidence over the behaviour of automated vehicles.

“I think there is something fundamentally different between a machine labelling an object as [a] person and us as humans, recognising an individual as being a person, someone who can get hurt, someone who has family, someone who has hopes and dreams.

“We need to be sure that the machines with which we share public space, and the companies that operate them, give us the same level of respect.”

Dr Reed said that even if driverless cars proved to be safer than vehicles driven by humans, there would still be mistakes and accidents, which could be very different from the types of incidents which humans had on the roads.

He said AI models would be constantly updated and improved based on real-life incidents.

“The crash that happens in Manchester on Monday should help to prevent a crash in Telford on Tuesday,” Dr Reed added.

data.title: London’s listed landlords have been unloved for too long
data.description:
Questor, The Telegraph’s investing column, takes a weekly view of the markets – what is moving them, what lies ahead and how all of this could affect your portfolios and financial goals. 

In the wake of the global financial crisis, the big banks traded at a discount for what seemed like forever. The market had given up on them and the narrative was working strongly against them.

This, of course, did not last forever.

During 2021 and 2024, the gold mining industry became a prime target for takeovers thanks to bargain-basement prices, and many deals were struck at a hefty premium.

This, too, did not last forever.

Today, one of the London Stock Exchange’s least loved sectors has a narrative working strongly against it: hefty discounts, lowly valuations and plenty of takeover interest.

So great is the interest that an £8bn bid by the Swiss Zurich Insurance for Lloyd’s of London syndicate manager Beazley is being drowned out by a paltry £150m deal.

Even though the Zurich bid equates to a 63pc premium to Beazley’s undisturbed, pre-bid share price, this column – and more than a few UK equity fund managers and analysts besides, based on recent conversations – are paying more attention to the smaller deal.

This is the cash-and-stock bid from British Land for Life Science Reit. At around £150m, representing a proposed 21pc takeover premium, the putative transaction is surely not as eye-catching.

But this is just the latest in a string of bids for UK real estate companies, and it may well be time to give the nation’s stock market-listed landlords a little more love.

Property plunge

Over the last five years, only nine of the 38 industrial sectors that comprise the FTSE 350 index have fallen in value. Real estate investment trusts (Reits) and real estate investment and services are two of them, with respective falls of 19pc and 21pc over that span.

(The best performer is aerospace and defence, with a 539pc capital gain, before dividends, followed by banks with a 232pc advance.)

It is easy to explain the wretched showing of Reits.

From an operational point of view, bricks-and-mortar retailers face an ongoing onslaught from online rivals to the detriment of demand for high street sites, while the rise of hybrid working is a threat to demand for offices. Meanwhile, the overall economic outlook remains murky.

The steady increase in interest rates between 2021 and 2023 may have crimped demand, hurt net asset value (Nav) calculations and made Reits’ dividend yields look less attractive relative to cash or Gilts.

These issues are all well known.

Less well known is that the property trade press is starting to report shortages of prime London office space. Interest rates still seem to be on a downward trajectory and Reits as a group trade at a sizeable discount to their latest reported Navs.

The current average discount across the sector is some 23pc, but the range is wide, from around zero, at Primary Health Properties and Sirius Real Estate, to more than 40pc at Unite Group, Workspace and Town Centre Securities.

The range partly reflects different target markets between the Reits – which is also why they require careful research – but the gap between price and value is attracting predators.

Sometimes these are trade buyers, as was the case when Belgium’s Shurgard bought Lok’n’Store in 2024, others are financial buyers, such as when investment group Blackstone snapped up Warehouse Reit in 2025.

There have been 13 bids made for London-listed real estate stocks in the past three years. The average bid premium is 24pc, with the highest north of 40pc. The average discount to historic Nav per share represented by those offers is just under 12pc, a figure much closer to asset value than the current sector valuation.

Mind the gap

Experienced investors know that buying a stock in the hope of a bid is a mug’s game. The real point is to buy well-run, well-financed businesses that have a strong competitive position at a sensible price, and tuck them away for the long term so dividends can be farmed (and if possible reinvested). Any takeover is simply a bonus.

But when the takeover activity is reminiscent of that seen in the gold mining industry during 2021 and 2024, and the lowly valuations also sound like an echo of the big banks, it would be foolish not to pay attention.

Eventually canny management teams started to buy back bank stock.

We are seeing the takeovers but not, intriguingly, the buybacks in the Reit sector. Perhaps Saba’s arrival on the share register of both Life Science Reit and Workspace will prompt further action, of one kind or another.

data.title: As a parent, I support child-free zones on trains – and in pubs
data.description:
The French rail company SNCF has sparked national outrage by advertising quieter “no kids” zones in select carriages on its trains.

All sides of the political compass frothed with indignant rage. One French conservative politician said “excluding children means excluding the future”. Another, on the far-Left, said that this “normalises the unacceptable”.

Under the weight of this barrage of pressure, SNCF withdrew the promotion and admitted it had used an “awkward marketing phrase” by referring to its business cabin as a child-free zone.

I appreciate that it is important to socialise children in public spaces so they can learn social rules. But I also don’t see anything particularly awkward about creating child-free (and child-friendly) zones within those public spaces.

The SNCF train move makes perfect sense, particularly where younger children are involved. As all parents know, even the most mild-mannered infant will struggle to sit still and stay quiet for long periods of time.

On the hour-long train journey into London, I find most fellow passengers will smile at my boy when he peers over the top of the seat. Many will engage in a game of peek-a-boo. Some will even move their feet to avoid tripping him up, as he marches up and down the aisle.

But on the odd occasion, for whatever reason, a train passenger is not in the mood and that is perfectly reasonable. Maybe they’re busy finishing a work presentation. Maybe they are hungover. Maybe they find being around young children difficult right now, for their own private reasons.

So yes. Given that “quiet” coaches on trains are roundly ignored, it makes sense to go a step further and have designated adult-only zones where children are outlawed. Ideally, these would be free, as I see no reason why people should have to pay for the privilege to not have a snot-filled child shouting “cow!” out of the window for their precious hour-long train journey.

There is precedent across the travel industry. There are now more than 1,000 hotels, B&Bs and holiday resorts which ban under 16s.

Cruise ships have cottoned on, too. Just last month, Oceania Cruises announced a “new era” of tranquility by only welcoming guests aged 18 years and older on its future sailings. Fred Olsen has made one of its ships, Borealis, adult-only and has dropped children’s activities from other ships. Ambassador will ban children on ex-UK sailings from this summer onwards.

In 2023, the Dutch airline Corendon Airlines launched an adults-only section separated by walls and curtains. Some pubs have trialled it, too. The Lower Red Lion in St Albans entered the spotlight for the sign outside its front door which read: “dog-friendly, child-free”. A post on X showing the sign received more than 75 million views, prompting the landlord to explain himself.

Landlord David Worcester told BBC Three Counties Radio he had “no objection to children in any other pub, ever, I just don’t want them in mine.”

Maybe it’s because our homes are too small. Maybe it’s because we are useless at saving and spend our disposable income on coffees and avocado on toast instead. Whatever the reason, millennial parents seem to spend more time in public spaces – cafes, restaurants, pubs – than our parents did. These have become our de facto living rooms. I am sure we travel more with young children, too.

My family fits this mould, but I do often worry that our little one is disturbing the peace when we’re out and about. If hospitality and transport firms made it clear where, exactly, families are welcome – and where they are not – this would bring peace of mind to parents. It could also mean facilities are better fitted for family needs. Likewise, adults looking for adult time should be entitled to that. Hell, some of them might even be parents who have been treated to an hour or two of peace.

So bring on the segregation. I am certain it would make for a happier society.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Traffic lights should turn green for cyclists, says Lime bike boss
data.description:
Traffic lights should automatically turn green for cyclists, according to the boss of Lime bikes.

Hal Stevenson, policy director for Lime, claimed it could reduce the number of cyclists ignoring red lights.

Transport for London (TfL) last month unveiled plans to give buses priority at all 3,500 traffic lights they pass in London by 2030.

Mr Stevenson called for the measure to be extended to bikes, saying: “Cyclists shouldn’t be left on red.”

Critics described the suggestion as “farcical”.

Mr Stevenson stated that TfL had extended London’s cycling network from about 90 km (56 miles) in 2016 to 430 km (267 miles) last year, with traffic light changes “the natural next step”.

“Rethinking how traffic lights are timed could be a natural next step in building on that success,” he wrote in an article for MyLondon. “Other leading cycling cities like Copenhagen and Amsterdam show what’s possible.

“Green-wave technology, which rewards cyclists travelling at a safe, consistent speed with a sequence of green lights, has improved safety and rider behaviour while reducing journey times at relatively low cost. It could also help address red-light running.”

He said traffic lights were currently timed “around motor traffic speeds”, which “led to frequent stopping for people on bikes”.

“Cycling now accounts for up to one in five trips in central London, with cycle traffic at peak times exceeding cars,” he added.

“Despite this, signal timings remain set around motor traffic speeds, which can lead to frequent stopping and less smooth journeys for people on bikes.”

Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association, said the policy would be “farcical” because London is “incredibly congested” with traffic “slower than it’s ever been”.

He claimed people who hire bikes are “incentivised” to take “dangerous risks” such as ignoring red lights because the companies’ time-based pricing means it is cheaper if journeys are completed more quickly.

Mr McNamara said he attended road safety meetings alongside members of the “white, middle-class cycling lobby” whose “whole obsession is removing vehicle traffic from the roads”.

He added: “Has it not occurred to them that some people either can’t or don’t want to cycle?

“It doesn’t mean there’s anything wrong with them.”

A recent report by location technology company TomTom found London was the world’s slowest capital city to drive in last year.

It took an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to drive one kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of London last year, according to the analysis.

This was partly blamed on widespread 20mph speed limits.

Reducing speed limits from 30mph to 20mph has been one of Sir Sadiq Khan’s key transport policies as part of his ambition to reduce road casualties.

A spokesman for the mayor said journey times on the TfL road network “remain at similar levels to those in 2019” and “studies have shown 20mph limits and zones may improve traffic flow and cut traffic jams”.

A TfL spokesman said: “We already take cyclists, as well as pedestrians and other traffic, into account when we review the timings of around 1,200 signals each year.

“We’re also working to transform the technology behind London’s traffic signals, moving towards an intelligent adaptive traffic signal system that optimises the amount of green time for everyone.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Farmers blame fat jabs as demand for potatoes slumps
data.description:
Weight-loss injections and a focus on healthy eating are killing off demand for potatoes, farmers have warned.

Potato growers said orders from supermarkets, manufacturers and fish and chip shops have slumped by as much as 10pc compared with a year ago as the soaring use of weight-loss injections suppresses consumer appetite for salty snacks and starchy foods.

Albert Bartlett, which is one of Britain’s biggest vegetable growers, pointed to industry data which showed sales by volume of fresh potatoes have fallen by 2.1pc over the past year.

The company said it had seen demand rise during the pandemic when more people were staying home to cook, but that this has fallen off in recent years.

Demand for frozen chips in particular has softened, the company said, adding that consumers were opting for “side of plate” options perceived as healthier than potatoes.

An estimated 2.5 million people are on weight-loss drugs, known as GLP-1s, which work by suppressing appetites and mimicking the feeling of fullness.

Many supermarkets and restaurants are adapting their food offerings to cater for the boom in fat jabs, with Marks & Spencer launching a “nutrient-dense” range and Morrisons rolling out “GLP-1 friendly” ready meals last year.

An industry report by Worldpanel compiled for food suppliers and seen by The Telegraph, found that people on weight-loss jabs were eating 46pc less “salty snacks”, such as crisps and salted nuts, versus those on weight-loss drugs. 

Consumers on GLP-1s also consumed 49pc less in sweet snacks, 71pc less in beer, wine and spirits and 45pc less in tobacco.

One large potato grower said demand among food producers and supermarkets was generally down between 5pc and 7pc on what it was last year. 

Meanwhile, some food manufacturers are understood to have cut requirements for “crisp” potatoes by as much as 10pc.

Rufus Pilgrim, of potato supplier RS Cockerill, said he was “most definitely” seeing weaker demand, with sales of varieties used for making crisps “quite considerably” lower.

He said: “There’s a reasonable percentage of the population on weight suppressant drugs, which are obviously cutting carbohydrate intake.”

Mr Pilgrim said the hit to crisping potatoes appeared to be part of Britain becoming “more of a health-conscious nation”.

Andrew Wace, a potato farmer based in Norfolk, said many consumers were opting for sweet potatoes over white versions, which tend to be imported from warmer regions such as Spain.

Another potato grower said the market was “definitely suffering from weaker demand”, adding that they also believed the recent decline in demand was down to rising use of weight-loss drugs in Britain, with a “big impact also coming in fried products”.

Fish and chip shops said the health shift was prompting them to offer smaller portions of menu items, including chips.

Andrew Crook, the president of the National Federation of Fish Friers, said: “I have customers that are using the jabs and they still come into my shop but buy smaller portions.”

Farmers are already grappling with a surplus of potatoes from bumper harvests, which has caused prices to plummet. According to industry newsletter Potato Call, Maris Piper potatoes are currently selling at around £180 a tonne, half of their price a year ago.

It is the latest setback for the rural industry after farmers were hit with an inheritance tax raid last year by the Government, which was subsequently watered down following a backlash from landowners.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Eco entrepreneur paid boarding school fees with company funds
data.description:
An eco-toothbrush entrepreneur used company money to fund her “affluent lifestyle”, including her children’s school fees, before the company folded, a court was told.

Sophie Perhar, 48, had challenged the decision by David Slinger, whose firm Synergy had backed her business financially, to put The Sustainable Bathroom Company Ltd into administration.

She claimed Mr Slinger, 73, called in administrators to “suffocate and kill” her company after she complained about his behaviour.

But a High Court judge dismissed Ms Perhar’s case after the court was told she had used funds meant for her company to sustain her lifestyle, including £85,000 for her children’s boarding school fees.

Mr Justice Green ruled that Mr Slinger had taken an “open-minded and constructive approach” to financially supporting Ms Perhar’s business, and that she brought disaster on herself.

The judge concluded Ms Perhar used Synergy as “a tool”, adding it was “useful for funding her good product, and in the event useful for assisting with her and her family’s affluent lifestyle”.

Ms Perhar launched her company in January 2019, and by early 2022, the company had a supply agreement for an electric bamboo toothbrush with Aldi.

In April of that year, Synergy agreed to lend the company up to £350,000 during a three-year period to meet manufacturing costs, in return for a 30 per cent share of profits.

Payments from Aldi for the toothbrushes were meant to go into an account controlled by Synergy. But the retailer was provided with the wrong bank details and more than €500,000 (£434,000) went into another account, controlled by Ms Perhar.

Ms Perhar then went on to pay out large sums from the account in March 2023 to meet her own “urgent” debts, including outstanding school fees and £40,000 to pawnbrokers.

Repayment of the loan

On June 5, 2023, Ms Perhar received a demand for repayment of the loan from Synergy, plus interest, based on the fact that she had breached the terms of her company’s borrowing agreement.

Three hours later, Synergy, as a secured creditor, put The Sustainable Bathroom Company Ltd into administration.

Ms Perhar claimed Synergy had “approved” her making the payments, telling the court she believed it had no legal right to put her company into administration, and that Mr Slinger was acting for “vindictive” and “improper” reasons.

She claimed Mr Slinger was angry after she complained to his female business partner of having been subjected to “uncomfortable talk about his personal life” during meetings.

Mr Slinger disputed the allegations, claiming his company is still out of pocket by about £375,000.

He was cleared of “improper motive” for appointing administrators, with Mr Justice Green ruling Ms Perhar caused her own company to fail through “unauthorised use” of business funds.

The judge added that Ms Perhar first became aware of the erroneous payments in February, but did not pass them to Synergy, meaning her contractual obligations had been “very largely ignored”.

data.title: Heineken cuts 6,000 jobs after slump in beer sales
data.description:
Heineken is to cut up to 6,000 jobs in response to waning beer demand amid younger people’s abstention.

The Dutch brewer, which also makes Amstel and Cruzcampo, said it would cut the roles over the next two years after a 2.1pc decline in beer volume sold in 2025. Revenues slumped by 4.7pc to €34.3bn (£29.9bn).

Heineken, which employs around 87,000 people globally, had already made more than €500m in savings last year, but it was not enough to avoid a 3.2pc decline in operating profits to €3.4bn.

Bosses said the company faced “subdued market conditions.”

Dolf van den Brink, the company’s chief executive, announced last month that he would step down in May as the company battles changing attitudes towards drinking – particularly among young people.

The Health Survey for England showed that 35pc of people aged between 16 and 24 did not drink any alcohol in 2024. That’s compared to 26pc in 2022. Record numbers of adults have gone teetotal, with 24pc now abstaining.

Rising costs

It underscores the challenge facing the beer and pub industry, which has also been hit by rising costs in Britain.

The hospitality sector in the UK has been grappling with surging business rates, while staff costs have also been pushed higher by rises to both employer National Insurance contributions and the minimum wage.

The Telegraph launched its Save Our Pubs campaign to raise awareness of the plight of embattled locals.

Heineken said it achieved only “low-single-digit” growth in sales of its zero and low-alcohol drinks.

But Heineken’s 0.0 Alcohol-Free Beer managed double-digit growth in 20 markets, with “notable strong performances in the USA, Canada and Spain”.

It plans to combat the shift in drinking habits by ramping up investment in “global brands, faster innovation and sharper execution”.

Mr Van den Brink said the company also expects its alcoholic drinks to rebound in the future.

“We remain prudent in the short term, and we remain confident in the mid to long-term that the category will return to growth,” he told Bloomberg TV.

Shares surged at their fastest pace in a year on Wednesday after the company announced its plans to cut 5,000 to 6,000 jobs, rising by as much as 5.5pc in early trading in Amsterdam.

Analysts Javier Gonzalez Lastra and Lavinia Norton at Berenberg said the beer maker’s results were “better than feared”.

Celine Pannuti of JP Morgan added that the market “had been worried” that the outlook would be worse following the upcoming departure of its boss.

Mr Van den Brink said that finding his successor was a “top priority” for the board.
