data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ocado’s dream of being the Tesla of groceries is unravelling fast
data.description:
When Tim Steiner retires, he would no doubt like to be remembered as Britain’s Elon Musk. After all, it was only recently that the Ocado founder was still breezily comparing the grocery outfit to Tesla.

There is no doubting Steiner’s entrepreneurial vision. But sadly, he increasingly risks being remembered as little more than a very good salesman.

Over the last 25 years, the founder of Ocado has repeatedly convinced some of the supposedly smartest people in the City and on Wall Street that his company is on the cusp of changing the face of supermarket shopping for good.

He has repeated this feat time and time again – persuading shareholders, bankers and the bond markets to lend Ocado vast sums of money to fulfil his dream of bringing online groceries to the masses.

If there was some sign of this coming true, you could perhaps understand their eagerness.

Since its inception, it is estimated that Ocado has raised more than £5.5bn – nearly three times its current stock-market value – from the equity and debt markets. Yet, when you consider that this is a business that has racked up £1.8bn of losses since going public in 2010, the enthusiasm is baffling

Perhaps the news that Ocado is planning as many as 1,000 job cuts as part of a scramble to rein in costs will convince the die-hards that Steiner’s “jam-tomorrow” story is destined to remain so forever.

The company has said little in response to reports of the cuts, other than that it regularly reviews its operations “to ensure we’re set up for long-term success,” – which is just silly. Ditto the idea that firing hundreds of people is somehow a demonstration of strength.

Instead, it smacks of an organisation running out of ideas and, potentially road. The truth is Ocado is in another spin, possibly its biggest yet, after several major customers watered down their ties with the Hatfield-based company.

Against that grim backdrop, all eyes are on Ocado’s annual results in a fortnight’s time after a 40pc slump in its share price in the last six months.

For Steiner to retain whatever credibility he has left after the disastrous events of recent months, the company needs to honour its promise to turn cash flow positive in the next financial year.

Its evangelical founder has said this is Ocado’s “core priority”, and full-year profitability could come shortly.

Yet with key customers scaling back their partnerships, it is undeniable that there is suddenly a sharper focus on what insiders describe as a commitment to “rigorous cost and capital discipline”.

Bernstein analyst William Woods predicted last month that there will now need to be “material cost-cutting” to the workforce, as well as the capital to invest in its supposedly world-beating technology.

At the same time, he pared his target share price to 100p, which is 58pc below the 229p level at which the stock closed on Monday.

Steiner’s grand vision has long been for Ocado to evolve from an online grocery delivery service to a technology provider that builds everything from consumer-facing websites to robot-operated depots for other food retailers – “the Tesla of grocery”, as he called it as recently as 2023.

It’s an intoxicating story that goes a long way to explaining why sophisticated institutional shareholders such as the hedge fund Roditi and money giant Schroders have been backers from day one.

However, the inconvenient truth that Steiner and his acolytes continue to overlook is that grocery deliveries remain unprofitable.

This is particularly true of the sort of hi-tech wizardry that Ocado provides.

The biggest problem facing the company is the vast amounts of upfront capital required to roll out large, robot-operated warehouses, the accompanying software and fleets of refrigerated vans – hence the need to repeatedly raise large sums of money from investors and the debt markets.

It is a model built on the premise that such outlays would eventually be recovered through lucrative long-term contracts with the biggest supermarkets worldwide, who would be happy to pay hefty licensing and consultancy fees for the privilege of using Ocado’s state-of-the-art technology.

For a while, it seemed a plausible scenario. Never more so than during the pandemic, when Steiner declared that “a dramatic and permanent shift towards online grocery shopping” was underway.

It was a boast that helped propel Ocado’s share price to the nosebleed heights of £29, and at one stage left the company worth more than double Sainsbury’s and Morrisons combined.

For now, management can still point to a long list of major customers, including Coles in Australia, Casino Group in France and ICA in Sweden. But for how long?

Kroger’s bombshell decision in November to close three of eight Ocado warehouses in the US was followed in January by an announcement from Canada’s Sobeys that it would close a distribution centre using Ocado’s kit.

The Kroger pullback on its own threatens to be pretty devastating to Ocado’s prospects.

The US chain is its most valuable customer by a long way – “not just half the embedded value, probably half the future value” – in the words of one retail chief. And its claim that the Ocado set-up only works in “higher-density” areas suggests serious limitations to the expansionary story it has been selling for years.

Ever the optimist, Steiner has attempted to spin the setback as an opportunity.

Under the Kroger tie-up, Ocado was prevented from doing deals with its rivals in the US and other international markets. Therefore, it is now free to seek other partners, the logic goes.

But with Sobeys quickly struck by buyer’s remorse too, surely the overwhelming question isn’t how many new customers Ocado can bring in but how many of its existing customers it can hold onto?

Steiner may be putting on a brave face, but a warning from Kroger that it will be keeping tabs on results at the remaining Ocado facilities must be giving him sleepless nights.

If his biggest customer is struggling to make the economics work, then surely the rest are?
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Worklessness ‘epidemic’ threatens the economy, warns Tesco
data.description:
Britain is “sleepwalking” into a worklessness epidemic with millions out of a job and on benefits, the boss of the nation’s biggest supermarket has warned.

Ashwin Prasad, Tesco’s UK chief, warned that economic growth was under threat because increasing numbers of Britons were choosing welfare over work.

Speaking at an event organised by the Resolution Foundation think tank in London, Mr Prasad said: “We have been sleepwalking into a quiet epidemic that is keeping millions of people out of work.

“My perspective from leading a major employer in this country is that far fewer people are in work than there could be.

“This means that instead of investing in parts of national life that might stimulate investment and growth into the wider economy, we are spending an ever-increasing proportion of our national income on out-of-work benefits.”

He also appeared to criticise increases in regulation and employer taxes, as he highlighted that the supermarket’s biggest cost was salaries and any change had a major impact.

His comments came on the same day as Sir Keir Starmer broke cover after nearly being ousted as Prime Minister by his own MPs.

‘Working-class’ PM

Sir Keir declared that he was a “working class” Prime Minister, in a signal that he will move to the Left amid pressure from Cabinet ministers and backbenchers.

In a fightback speech, the Prime Minister said when he was growing up his family “didn’t have enough money to pay our bills” and struggled to make ends meet.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, a former Labour leader and an ally of Sir Keir, said on Tuesday that the Prime Minister was personally angered by the “class divide”.

Left-wing Labour MPs are using Sir Keir’s moment of political weakness to press for more socialist policies, with one telling The Telegraph: “A complete change is imperative.”

Meanwhile Andy Burnham, the Mayor of Greater Manchester, who harbours leadership ambitions, called for councils to get greater powers to repossess poor-quality privately rented homes.

Whether Sir Keir sticks by his plans for welfare reform, and announces them in time for Budget this autumn, could become a key policy battleground.

Mr Prasad highlighted official forecasts that showed Britain was on course to spend £330bn on welfare this year alone, including £83.1bn on sickness and disability benefits that he suggested was unsustainable.

On Tuesday, MPs approved a 3.8pc increase in benefit spending, which Stephen Timms, the work and pensions minister, said “commits the Government to increased expenditure of £9bn in 2026/27”.

Tesco is one of Britain’s largest private sector employers with 300,000 staff. Mr Prasad said the supermarket was always seeking to recruit untapped potential and was working with government to support thousands of people to work flexibly including veterans and carers.

However, he added that there was a smaller pool of talent to choose from as more people went on benefits.

“We cannot afford to be a country that lets the next generation languish on the sideline,” the executive said.

The Tesco chief also took aim at excessive regulation, implying that rising costs were making it harder for the supermarket to hire people.

He said: “Each time you add a new cost, money has to come from somewhere. In the past five years, we’ve already seen all sorts of new costs for labour, costs for energy and costs for regulation.”

The warning from Mr Prasad, who became Tesco’s UK chief executive last year, adds to growing alarm about the scale of the worklessness crisis.

There are currently 2.8 million people of working age who are not even looking for a job because of a long-term health problem. The number of young people who are neither in work nor education has risen to almost one million people.

Alan Milburn, the former health secretary who is leading a review into the surge in youth worklessness over the past decade, recently told The Telegraph that the crisis was sending Britain to “hell in a handcart”.

While Mr Prasad did not offer a hypothesis of the main driver behind the rise in worklessness, he said a clear trend had developed over the past decade.

Britain’s welfare bill as a share of the economy is set to keep rising until the end of parliament, according to official forecasts.

This has been driven by a surge in disability benefit claims known as Personal Independence Payment (PIP), as well as sickness benefits that do not require people to look for work. While PIP is not an out of work benefit, the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) has previously said only around one in six claimants have a job.

Sir Keir has lost his chief of staff Morgan McSweeney, the architect of his strategy of reaching for the centre ground, in a wider overhaul of his No 10 team.

The Prime Minister survived calls on Monday from Anas Sarwar, the Scottish Labour leader, to quit only after Cabinet ministers rallied to his side, buying him time.

Sir Keir met with Cabinet ministers for a political meeting on Tuesday which lasted one hour and 40 minutes, much longer than usual.

One Cabinet minister told The Telegraph of the discussions: “We all listened and resolved to be more open and challenging with each other.”

Speaking in public for the first time after the turmoil, Sir Keir talked about how important the cost of living crisis was to Britons.

Sir Keir went on: “I know that’s what matters most from personal experience. When I was growing up we didn’t have enough money to pay our bills and I know what it feels like to sit round the kitchen table worrying about the fact that you can’t make ends meet. And that’s what people are doing across the country and it’s our job to change that.

“But I also know it’s the most important issue that we must focus on because I know what I’m fighting for and who I’m fighting for. I’ve got the most working-class Cabinet in the history of this country sitting round my Cabinet table and I’m really proud of that.

“Really proud of what they bring to the table, their personal journeys to get there, their experiences. But it is utter nonsense to suggest that that means everybody gets a fair chance in life, utter nonsense. I feel very strongly about this.

“I’m in that Cabinet as Prime Minister, having come from a working-class background to be Prime Minister of this country. But that’s the exception, that is not the rule. Even within my own family, my brother, who died last year, he had difficulties learning when he was growing up. He spent his adult life wandering from job to job in virtual poverty.

‘Millions in the same boat’

“This political system didn’t work for him and there are millions of people in the same boat.”

The claim about the Cabinet being the most “working class” in history appeared to be a reference to how every Cabinet minister went to state school.

The Prime Minister rarely talks explicitly about his “working class” background in public, though he has frequently mentioned in speeches how his father was a toolmaker.

The remarks came just hours after Mr Miliband himself referenced class differences and indicated the Prime Minister was willing to take a more “bold” approach.

Mr Miliband told BBC Radio 4: “I tell you what angers Keir most about this country, it’s class. It’s the class divide.

“One of the things I’ve been talking about this whole period is saying more about that ... He exists to change that.”

Mr Burnham on Tuesday also set out what he would do if he was in Government.

Speaking at a Resolution Foundation conference in London, he said: “We should give powers and funding to councils to purchase compulsory homes in the private rented sector which are non-decent.

“You should no longer get the right in this country to rent out a home with the help of the benefits system when the condition of that home harms the health of the residents within it and drags down the whole community around it.”

A spokesman from the Department for Work and Pensions said: “We share the ambition to get more people into work and are taking decisive action - investing £1.5 billion in work, training and apprenticeships, and supporting 300,000 people with disabilities into employment through Connect to Work.

“We’re working hand in hand with employers through Sir Charlie Mayfield’s Keep Britain Working Review and Alan Milburn is investigating the root causes of youth worklessness.

“Through our Jobs Guarantee we are helping young people find paid work placements, and we are grateful to the dozens of employers who have already expressed an interest in the scheme - including Tesco.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Energy giant revives wind farm after Miliband’s £1.8bn subsidy blitz
data.description:
A Danish energy giant has revived plans for a massive North Sea wind farm after Ed Miliband promised billions more in subsidies.

Ørsted, the world’s largest wind farm operator, said it would resubmit plans for the Hornsea 4 wind farm off the east of England next year after Mr Miliband offered a much higher subsidy for the power generated.

The group blew a hole in the Energy Secretary’s net zero hopes last year by cancelling the project. It said government-backed grants under the Contracts for Difference (CfD) scheme were too low.

Mr Miliband last month announced plans to boost the annual subsidies on offer, promising them minimum average prices of £95 per megawatt hour, which is about £10 more than Ørsted would have received under a prior deal.

Another decision by Mr Miliband means the contracts guaranteeing the payments, estimated by the energy department to cost up to £1.8bn a year, were also extended from 15 years to 20 years.

Hornsea 4 is a 180-turbine project off the coasts of Norfolk and Yorkshire. It would have a capacity of 2.4 gigawatts, among the largest ever built, generating enough power for 2.6 million homes on a windy day.

Ørsted’s rethink will be welcomed by Mr Miliband. When he originally approved the project he heralded it as a key step towards decarbonising Britain’s electricity grid by 2030.

At the time, the Government had guaranteed developers £85 per megawatt hour as a minimum price for any power generated.

Wholesale power is usually well below this price so the Government makes up the difference, funding the costs via a levy on consumer and business bills.

At the time it was widely predicted across the energy industry that the price would have to rise or Mr Miliband’s 2030 target would never be met.

That belief prompted some developers to hold back schemes in the hope of getting extra subsidy – a view that has since proven to be correct.

The Office for Budget Responsibility predicts that the cost of CfD contracts for renewable generators are set to rise from £2.3bn to around £6bn by the end of the decade once the cost of new wind farms is included. All such costs will be met via a levy on power bills.

The confirmation of the restart coincided with Ørsted’s annual financial results showing a big hit on its US operations because of Donald Trump’s attack on wind farms.

The US president has claimed the machines kill whales and birds and their largely foreign ownership is a threat to national security.

Rasmus Errboe, Ørsted chief executive, said scrapping the UK wind farm last year was a sign of the company’s “disciplined approach” to spending.

“During 2025, we demonstrated this disciplined approach in relation to Hornsea 4, which we are now reconfiguring for potential future development … we continue to hold the seabed lease, grid connection, and key permits.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Net zero backlog puts Labour’s clean energy target at risk, warns Ofgem
data.description:
Wind and solar farms seeking to connect to Britain’s electricity grid are facing “widespread delays” in a major blow to Labour and Ed Miliband’s net zero targets.

Ofgem, the energy regulator, has warned that 210 of the 340 green energy projects scheduled to connect to the grid in 2026 and 2027 are set to miss the target.

Massive amounts of wind, solar and battery capacity must be added to the system in order to hit the Energy Secretary’s target to switch Britain’s grid to 95pc clean power by 2030.

As recently as November 2024, the Government and Ofgem said they could not “stress highly enough” the importance of connecting projects slated for 2026 and 2027 on time.

But in its letter published on Friday, Ofgem said officials were “frustrated and disappointed” to discover that hundreds of wind, solar and battery projects “imperative to ensure clean power by 2030” were already late.

The regulator blamed network companies responsible for connecting the systems together, such as the National Grid, for the majority of the delays – with long waits for planning permission and supply chain issues contributing to some of the setbacks.

In a letter to the energy industry, Ofgem said it had already rejected a request from the National Energy System Operator (Neso) and the likes of National Grid to push back the connection date for some projects after they had asked for more time.

The regulator claimed that two-thirds of the delays were “either network-driven or both network- and developer-driven”.

‘Zombie’ projects

Companies hit back and blamed the slow pace of reforms to the system of connecting projects to the electricity grid.

The Government has been working for years with Ofgem and Neso to speed up connections. An outdated “first come, first serve” approach has left the queue clogged up with “zombie” projects that are not ready, delaying those that are.

Reforms were unveiled last year to reorder the queue so that projects closest to completion are given priority.

Mr Miliband’s clean power action plan, published in December 2024, said these changes were “critical” to Labour’s clean power 2030 mission and that without them “projects we need will be delayed.”

However, Ofgem said in its letter that Neso and National Grid had warned in June 2025 that there was a risk some projects would not be able to connect on time. This was then followed by a formal request to delay them in October.

In its letter, Ofgem criticised Neso and network companies for providing “insufficient information” on both the scale of the delays and their causes.

Ofgem said it was also carrying out an investigation into the delays and why they have occurred.

Neso said it shared Ofgem’s disappointment and was “working at pace” to deliver faster connection times.

A spokesman for Ofgem said: “We are working closely with Neso and the energy sector to help achieve the Government’s 2030 clean power goals.

“Last year, we approved a range of reforms to the electricity grid connections process so ready and needed projects can connect more rapidly and help unlock economic growth.

“We are disappointed that these delays have occurred and are continuing to investigate the causes and scale of the issues identified.”

On Monday, National Grid claimed the delays were “not surprising” because the Government’s grid connection reforms did not take effect immediately.

A spokesman said: “By exempting so many projects from the process in the first two years, the reforms are not truly coming into effect until 2028.

“We are now working with Neso, the Government, Ofgem and other networks to unlock the benefits of a reformed connections process for the future, delivering new connections for customers as quickly as possible, while protecting our existing and future customers from the costs of building more capacity than is needed.”

data.title: BP scraps £550m shareholder payout in scramble to cut debts
data.description:
BP has pledged to “do better” after cancelling a $750m (£549m) shareholder payout as it scrambles to cut debts.

The FTSE 100 giant, which helps fund the pensions of millions of Britons through dividends, scrapped its share buyback programme on Tuesday after battening down the hatches following turbulence at the group.

It will seek to cut billions more in costs in 2026 alongside scrapping the buyback to reduce its debt pile 

The oil giant is struggling under the weight of a $22bn debt mountain and has suffered recent turmoil following the sudden exit of its chief executive Murray Auchincloss after less than two years in post.

Albert Manifold, BP’s chairman who was instrumental in Mr Auchincloss’s exit, has hired Woodside Energy chief Meg O’Neill as his replacement.

On Tuesday, Carol Howle, BP’s interim chief executive, attempted to placate investors by saying the company would seek to improve its performance.

“We are in action and we can and will do better for our shareholders,” she said.

BP’s latest $750m buyback programme was announced in November 2025 before Tuesday’s suspension.

BP said it had sunk to a $3.4bn loss in the three months ending in December, compared to a $1.2bn profit in the third quarter of 2025, amid weaker crude oil prices.

Its underlying replacement costs profit – its preferred measure of profitability – fell from $8.9bn to $7.5bn for the year. Its replacement costs profit fell 16pc over the quarter, in line with analysts forecasts.

The latest fall in profitability included an impairment charge of $4bn on its “low carbon energy” division, part of a costly write-down of BP’s net zero bets disclosed in January.

BP had expected the oil price to be around $76 a barrel for this year but it currently trades at around $69, adding pressure to the company’s finances.

“With a continued emphasis on capital discipline and returns, we are reducing capital expenditure for 2026 to the lower end of the guidance range, while continuing to drive down our cost base,” Ms Howle said.

BP last year tore up a strategy, first introduced by former boss Bernard Looney in 2020, to focus on renewables and the green energy transition after a backlash from shareholders. This included scrapping a multi-billion pound hydrogen plant on Teesside.

The oil giant had previously intended to cut its oil output by 40pc and reach net zero emissions by 2050.

The arrival in April of Ms O’Neill, a former executive at US oil major ExxonMobil, is expected to trigger renewed focus on BP’s fossil fuels division.

BP is already increasing its oil and gas investment and exploration. It is also in the process of selling $20bn of assets.

Derren Nathan, an analyst at Hargreaves Lansdowne, said the decision to scrap BP’s share buyback was part of an attempt to “clear the decks” ahead of the arrival of Ms O’Neill as it pursues a “leaner, meaner” strategy.

Shares in BP fell 3.5pc in early trading.

data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.

data.title: Starmer is putting his political survival ahead of the national interest
data.description:
Back in 2015, during the so-called “golden era” of Sino-British relations, Xi Jinping was welcomed to Britain for a full state visit.

Between the pomp and ceremony, he was taken to the University of Manchester facility where graphene – an extremely strong and thin material – had been invented.

The Government wanted to showcase to Xi what we were capable of and persuade him to open China’s huge markets to our products. By all accounts, Xi left impressed.Partnerships with China’s Engine Aero Corporation and Central South University soon followed.

The problem, however, was that he left far too impressed. Years later, it emerged that Beijing had sent a student to study at our National Graphene Institute. After finishing his PhD, he transferred to Beijing’s National University of Defence Technology and worked on key projects for the Chinese military. Soon after, China’s latest attack helicopters were fitted with graphene armour.

The naivety ended up backfiring economically too. Although graphene was invented in Britain, fewer than 2 per cent of graphene-related patents are registered here today. Around 60 per cent are registered in China.

The idea that engagement would influence China in a more positive direction was no less misguided. On his visit Xi was gifted a box set of Sherlock, then a firm British favourite on Chinese TV, and sunk pints at the Plough pub near Chequers.

But the fake bonhomie proved utterly pointless. The Golden Era was followed by oppression in Hong Kong, mass incarceration in Xinjiang and massive increases in military activity around Taiwan.

Starmer knows the dark reality of our relationship with China better than anybody. He will be regularly briefed by our security agencies on their industrial-scale espionage. He will have read the reports on their currency manipulation, dumping and intellectual property theft. And he has seen the way China weaponises economic integration to bully our allies who refuse to submit to them. When Australia rightly called for an investigation into the origins of Covid-19, Beijing slapped them with crippling tariffs.

So, why, given all this, has Starmer gone cap in hand to Beijing? The short answer is he is desperate.

His blunt calculation is that he needs their money. And he needs it now. The Chancellor’s coffers are empty. Growth has ground to a halt. He’s after some private investment that might inch up his meagre growth figures by a few decimal points.

Fighting for his personal survival, he cares not one jot about the long-term consequences.

Undermined our Armed Forces

Of course, improving his own lot at the expense of his country’s long-term security is nothing new for this Prime Minister. His legal career was founded upon so-called human rights claims that undermined our Armed Forces. He claims he was obliged to accept such work but as The Telegraph revealed this week, he worked with the disgraced solicitor Phil Shiner for free.

His work “challeng[ing] the government over the reach of its anti-terror legislation and taken on the British military”, according to The Guardian, led to his appointment as Director of Public Prosecutions and subsequent political career.

The question is, will Starmer be allowed to repeat the trick of enhancing his own career at the expense of his country’s interests?

To boost our country’s growth and living standards, the Prime Minister doesn’t even need to fly to Beijing to offer himself as tribute.

One of the fallacies of globalisation was that manufacturing could be outsourced to the East, but Britain could remain a powerhouse of innovation. The idea was that companies like Dyson could make their products overseas, but bright sparks here could continue to design them all thousands of miles from the production line. It doesn’t work like that. The two are too inextricably linked, as every Western economy has now learnt to its cost.

Starmer needs what Nigel Farage and Reform are championing: reindustrialisation. Since 2003 our heavy industry has halved. It’s left our economy weak, lopsided and vulnerable. It’s this industrial malaise that underlies much of our decline.

The maladies behind it are well rehearsed in these pages: the highest energy prices in the developed world, sclerotic planning, and insane net zero targets to name but a few. Ending these drag anchors on our economy and returning to making and building things would require the Prime Minister to stand up to those he fears far more than a dictator; Ed Miliband and his backbenchers.

The path back to growth lies not in Manchurian capitulation but Mancunian innovation. We still have some of the most innovative and entrepreneurial people in the world. We just need a Government that will see them, not our adversaries, as the key to our future.

data.title: Boohoo tycoon must have known about Leicester sweatshops, lawyers claim
data.description:
The co-founder of Boohoo is likely to have known about the “terrible, unsafe and unsanitary conditions” in Leicester sweatshops that supplied his business before the scandal came to light, a High Court lawsuit has claimed.

Mahmud Kamani, who started the online clothes seller in 2006, was probably aware that the factories Boohoo bought its clothes from were underpaying workers and subjecting them to “arbitrary abuse and humiliation,” lawyers claimed.

“It is not, the claimants say, credible that Mr Kamani was unaware of the conditions in which those factories were being operated,” the lawsuit says.

“Boohoo denies the alleged knowledge of its board. The claimants say that these denials are not convincing.”

The claim emerged in a lawsuit in which investors are seeking £177m plus interest from Boohoo in compensation for a share price crash in 2020.

Boohoo’s stock fell by 42pc after press reports revealed workers at Leicester factories that supply it were paid “significantly less than the minimum wage” with many on as little as £3 per hour. Staff were also working long hours in cramped conditions and dilapidated buildings.

City law firm Fox Williams, representing the investors, claims Mr Kamani, Boohoo’s executive chairman, had long-standing relationships with the owners of the Leicester sweatshops, attended social events with some of the suppliers and even attended some of the factories himself.

Lawyers are now pushing for Mr Kamani to hand over logs of his communications with two of his sons, Umar and Samir, on the basis that those messages likely contain relevant information.

The skeleton argument says Mr Kamani “is highly likely to have discussed relevant matters with his sons,” while noting both Umar and Samir Kamani held positions in the company as the heads of major brands.

Labour rights activists claimed that factory staff were also expected to come into work while sick with Covid-19 at the height of the pandemic, when Leicester was under stringent lockdown restrictions.

Controversy wiped £1bn off value

The controversy wiped more than £1bn off of Boohoo’s value following the plunge in the company’s share price on London’s Alternative Investment Market. Boohoo subsequently launched a £55m takeover of Debenhams in 2021 and rebranded under the British department store’s name.

The lawsuit says Boohoo failed to “take its social responsibility seriously” and “did not strive for high standards in ethics throughout its supply chain,” prior to the scandal being unveiled.

It points out that the wider Kamani family also held 37pc of all shares in Boohoo, while pointing to claims the listed business had “vestiges and traits of a family business.”

It says that the Kamani family had links to some of the Leicester factories and that some of the suppliers were even introduced to Boohoo by Mr Kamani’s brother, Jalal, the company’s former trading director.

In response, lawyers for Boohoo, from Herbert Smith Freehills, said: “There is no reasonable basis to include members of the Kamani family other than Mahmud Kamani,” while noting none of them held senior leadership positions inside the fast-fashion retailer.

Boohoo said it “strongly contests the allegations and will vigorously defend any claim.”

The investors include the California State Teachers’ Retirement System, or CalSTRS, which manages over $390bn (£285bn) worth of pensions.

data.title: Chinese EVs will destroy jobs unless Government acts, supplier warns
data.description:
European nations risk losing millions of jobs unless they stem the flood of cheap electric cars from China, the new owner of UK car parts supplier Dowlais has warned.

David Dauch, the chief executive of US-based Dauch Corp, said that an influx of electric vehicles (EVs) from the Far East threatens to wipe out vast swathes of suppliers in the West.

He said: “Countries are going to have to put the right policies in place to make sure it’s a level playing field for the suppliers and manufacturers in each of those countries.

“If not, then they run the risk of losing jobs and potentially losing industries.”

Europe’s automotive industry employs about 14m people across carmakers and suppliers.

Mr Dauch said Europe will have to consider whether to follow Donald Trump in effectively blocking Chinese EVs from the US through a combination of 100pc tariffs and strict regulations.

He said: “Right now, they are banned from coming into the US because of national security and other reasons.

“The US has put certain things in place, tariffs or other measures, to prevent them from coming in because we’re trying to protect the manufacturing base and jobs in our country.

“Other countries have to assess that on an individual basis and make a decision on what’s best for their citizens and the GDPs in each of their economies.”

Almost 28pc of the EVs sold in Britain last year were Chinese following an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda.

Dauch Corp’s £1.16bn purchase of GKN Automotive’s holding company, Dowlais, will create a global leader in components that transmit power to a car’s wheels such as driveshafts, differentials and axles.

The US firm, which changed its name from American Axle last month, caters mostly to the internal combustion engine vehicles that remain dominant in North America.

GKN – which will be known by its original name following the abolition of the Dowlais brand – is also a leader in EV drive systems.

Mr Dauch said the enlarged firm will adopt an “agnostic” stance on EVs versus traditional vehicles, claiming that the rush to move on from petrol and diesel engines had been overdone.

He said: “Several countries have tried to move too fast when they weren’t properly set up to support that.

“I think the UK was one of those and the US was one of those. Things were politically motivated. There are certain ways that people are trying to drive policy.

“Many countries have already gone back to review their initial policies, several have already made a decision to push those out, others have not. I think it’s going to be dependent on what the impact is on the manufacturing base for each country.”

Mr Dauch said that the Trump administration’s elimination of EV incentives had caused its market share fall from a peak of 10pc to around 5pc.

He said that makes it extremely unlikely that battery, plug-in hybrid and fuel-cell vehicles will capture 50pc of US sales by 2030, as targeted by Joe Biden, the former US president.

“I’m not saying it won’t happen over time but the time is going to be much longer than people think,” he said.

Mr Dauch said that while EVs are doing better in Europe, expecting the average driver to switch to models that cost more and have a limited range and insufficient charging infrastructure was to deny market forces.

He said: “The consumer is the boss and the only way they are going to buy a vehicle is if it’s affordable, safe and they can charge it.”

The Dowlais name was adopted in 2023 when GKN Automotive was spun off from GKN Aerospace by owner Melrose.

data.title: Royal favourite Frog Bikes facing administration
data.description:
A children’s bike-maker favoured by Prince Louis is facing an uncertain future after filing an intention to appoint administrators.

Frog Bikes, founded in 2013 by husband-and-wife team Jerry and Shelley Lawson, made a court application through its law firm Shoosmiths on Friday to hire administrators, legal filings show.

Filing an intention to appoint an administrator is a legal step taken by companies to gain temporary protection from creditors. It can lead to a full-blown liquidation of a company or reorganisation of its finances.

Frog Bikes said the move was a “precautionary measure” to allow the company to keep trading while it explores “potential funding and restructuring solutions”.

Mr Lawson blamed the predicament on a “series of significant and well-documented challenges” – including higher employee taxes, Brexit, and low consumer confidence.

He added, “At the same time, tighter financial markets and reduced access to funding made it increasingly difficult for growing manufacturing businesses to secure the requisite working capital required to navigate these challenges.”

‘Continuing friction’ from Brexit

The Wales-based group has gained a cult following and won a dream endorsement when the Princess of Wales published a photo of her son riding one of the company’s products on his third birthday.

The business makes child-specific bikes after the founders said they struggled to find suitable ones for their own children.

Its lightweight aluminium bikes are made in Pontypool, Wales, where Mr and Mrs Lawson opened a 120,000-square-foot manufacturing facility.

After its success in the UK, it quickly became a market leader for affordable children’s bikes and rapidly expanded to more than 50 countries – including the US and Hong Kong.

However, Frog Bikes has recently warned that cumbersome export taxes were eating into profits.

In 2023, it lost £530,000 after blaming “continuing friction” from Brexit, which had made it harder to compete with European rivals.

However, in its most recent accounts for the year to February 2024, auditors said the group would continue as a going concern.

The report added that despite inflation falling at the time, consumers were still cautious in their spending on “big ticket purchases”, adding that there was competition from new entrants to the lightweight children’s bike market.

Bike market in turmoil

Cycling businesses have faced a difficult time as enthusiasm for the activity starts to wane.

A pandemic boom led many companies to expand rapidly, but after the interest faded, firms complained that the sudden drop in interest had left manufacturers with excess inventory.

Companies like Brompton Bicycles saw profits fall by more than 99pc in the year to March 2024. The following year, it reported pre-tax losses of £2m. At the time, the company described the bike market as being “in turmoil”.

Raleigh, another British brand, reported a loss of £30m in 2023, citing overstocked inventory following the pandemic.

Frog Bikes confirmed in a statement that the search for a new investor would continue.

Mr Lawson said: “All parties are committed to securing a positive outcome for the business, its employees and wider stakeholders.

“Frog Bikes’ priority remains supporting its employees, customers, suppliers, and retail partners while working with its advisers to determine the best possible outcome for all stakeholders.”
